CITY OF SAN MATEO
City Council Regular Meeting
May 20, 2024
7:00 PM
City Hall Council Chamber 330 W.
20th Avenue San Mateo CA 94403
Entrance is on O’Farrell Street
COUNCIL MEMBERS
Lisa Diaz Nash, Mayor
Rob Newsom Jr., Deputy Mayor
Rich Hedges
Amourence Lee
Adam Loraine
 
AGENDA
THIS MEETING CAN BE ATTENDED IN PERSON OR REMOTE BY JOINING ZOOM – SEE CODES BELOW.
PUBLIC COMMENTS WILL BE ACCEPTED BOTH IN PERSON AND REMOTELY.
SEE END OF AGENDA FOR OPTIONS AND INSTRUCTIONS ON HOW TO PARTICIPATE IN THE MEETING. 
To join via Zoom – click here: May 20, 2024
To join via telephone:  (408) 638-0968      
Webinar ID: 830 4437 1207      Passcode: 521285
The City Council meeting will conclude by 11:00 p.m. unless otherwise extended by council vote. 
Any unheard items will automatically move forward to the next regular meeting. 
CALL TO ORDER
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll Call
AB 2449 Requests and Considerations for Meeting Participation by Teleconference due to Just Cause or Emergency
Circumstances
CEREMONIAL
1.
Public Works Week – Proclamation
2.
Mental Health Month – Proclamation
3.
Older Americans Month – Proclamation
CONSENT CALENDAR
All matters listed under the Consent Calendar are considered by the Council to be routine and will be enacted by one motion without
discussion. If discussion is desired, that item may be removed and considered separately.
 
4.
City Council Meeting Minutes – Approval
Approve the minutes of the special and regular meetings of April 1, 2024 and April 15, 2024.
CEQA: This minutes approval is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative
activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section
15378(b)(5).)
1 of 2271

--- page 2 ---
5.
San Jose Student Intern – Agreement
Approve a no-cost agreement through April 26, 2029 with San Jose State University for placement of an intern
and ratify execution of the agreement in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity
that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)
(5).)
6.
Gasoline Fuel Procurement and Delivery – Amendment
Approve Amendment No. 5 to the agreement with Pinnacle Petroleum, Inc. for gasoline fuel procurement and
delivery to extend the term through June 30, 2025; increase the agreement by an amount not to exceed
$515,000, for a new total agreement amount of $2,390,000; establish a contingency reserve in the amount of
$100,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the amendment in substantially the form
presented and issue amendments within the contingency amount.
CEQA: Approval of Amendment No. 5 is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a continuing administrative or
maintenance activity involving the purchase of supplies. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(2).)
7.
Diesel Fuel Procurement and Delivery Services – Amendment
Approve Amendment No. 5 to the agreement with Bosco Oil, Inc. dba Valley Oil Company for diesel fuel
procurement and delivery to extend the term through June 30, 2025; increase the agreement amount by an
amount not to exceed $310,000, for a new total agreement amount of $1,435,000; establish a contingency
reserve in the amount of $75,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the amendment in
substantially the form presented and issue amendments within the contingency amount.
CEQA: Approval of Amendment No. 5 is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a continuing administrative or
maintenance activity involving the purchase of supplies. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(2).)
8.
Citywide Surface Treatment Packages 4A-1 and 4A-2 – Agreement
Approve an agreement with HDR Engineering, Inc. for design professional services for the Citywide Surface
Treatment Packages 4A-1 and 4A-2 in an amount not to exceed $584,000; establish a contingency reserve of
$58,400; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented
and approve amendments, including no-cost time extensions, within the contingency amount.
CEQA: This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation,
repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures,
facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or
former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
9.
Citywide Street Rehabilitation Package 6B – Agreement
Approve an agreement with Nichols Consulting Engineers, Inc. for design professional services for the Citywide
Street Rehabilitation Package 6B project in an amount not to exceed $820,000; establish a contingency reserve of
$82,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented
and approve amendments, including no-cost time extensions, within the contingency amount.
CEQA: This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation,
repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures,
facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or
former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
2 of 2271

--- page 3 ---
10.
Train Horn Noise Quiet Zone Projects – Service Agreement
Approve a Service Agreement with the Peninsula Corridor Joint Powers Board for the Train Horn Noise Quiet Zone
projects at Villa Terrace and East Bellevue Avenue in the amount of $59,440; establish a contingency reserve in
the amount of $9,000; and authorize the Director of Public Works to execute the Agreement in substantially the
form presented and approve amendments within the contingency amount.
CEQA: Approval of a Service Agreement between the City and the PCJPB is not a project subject to CEQA, because
it can be seen with certainty that there is no possibility that the review of the Improvements design intended to
minimize train horn noise may have a significant effect on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)
(3).)
11.
Bermuda Drive Utility Relocation Project – Agreement
Adopt a Resolution to approve an agreement with Pacific Gas & Electric for the Bermuda Drive Utility Relocation
Project in the amount of $186,685.48; and authorize the Director of Public Works to execute the Agreement in
substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation,
repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures,
facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or
former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
12.
Summer Children’s and Youth Theater Program – Agreement
Adopt a Resolution to approve an alternative purchasing procedure and approve an agreement with Bay Area
Educational Theater Company to provide children’s and youth theater programs during the summer camp
programing from June 16, 2024 through August 31, 2025 in an amount not to exceed $254,000; and authorize
the Director of Parks and Recreation to execute the agreement and approve change orders up to $25,000 in
substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause
a physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
13.
2nd & El Camino Garage Improvements – Amendment
Approve Amendment No. 2 with ZFA Structural Engineers for additional structural investigation services at the
2nd and El Camino Garage in the amount of $45,201.50, for a revised total amount of $197,441.50; and authorize
the Public Works Director to execute the amendment in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation,
repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures,
facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or
former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
14.
Casanova Park Restroom Remodel – Contingency Increase
Approve an increase to the contingency reserve associated with the Newton Construction & Management, Inc.
construction contract for the Casanova Park Restroom Remodel in the amount of $75,000, for a revised total
contingency reserve of $120,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to issue change orders within the
contingency amount.
CEQA: This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation,
repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures,
facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or
3 of 2271

--- page 4 ---
former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
15.
Police Department and City Clerk’s Office – Classification Updates
Adopt a Resolution to 1) add 1.0 Full Time Equivalent Senior Management Analyst position in the Police
Department; 2) update the job specification for the classification of Senior Management Analyst; 3) increase the
salary range for the Deputy City Clerk classification; and 4) update the job specification for the classification of
Deputy City Clerk.
CEQA: These classification updates are not subject to CEQA, because they are government fiscal activities that will
not cause a physical change in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
16.
Emergency Communications Equipment – Indemnity Agreement
Approve an indemnity agreement between the City and the San Mateo County Pre-Hospital Emergency Medical
Services Group; and authorize the City Manager to execute the indemnity agreement in substantially the form
presented.
CEQA: The approval of an indemnity agreement between the City and the JPA allowing access to upgrade public
safety related communications equipment is not subject to CEQA review under the “common sense” exemption
because it can be seen with certainty that there is no possibility that this activity may have a significant effect on
the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3).)
PUBLIC COMMENT
Members of the public wishing to comment on any item not appearing on the agenda may address the City Council at this time. State
law prevents Council from taking action on any matter not on the agenda; your comments may be referred to staff for follow up.
Public comment is limited to a total of 15 minutes; however, an opportunity for additional public comment may be provided later in
the agenda.
 
PUBLIC HEARING
City Council action on the following public hearing(s) represents a final decision concluding all administrative proceedings. Judicial
review may be had only if a petition is filed with the Court not later than the 90th day following the date the decision is made.
Judicial review of environmental determinations may be subject to a time period for litigation as short as 30 days following the date
the decision is made.
 
17.
General Plan Amendment to Adopt San Mateo's Revised 2023-2031 Housing Element
Adopt a resolution to approve a General Plan Amendment to adopt the Revised Housing Element for the Sixth
Housing Cycle (2023-2031) that is compliant with State law, provides a plan for at least 10,286 new housing units,
and affirmatively furthers fair housing, and is exempt from review under the California Environmental Quality Act,
based on the identified findings.
CEQA: The Revised Housing Element is not subject to further review under CEQA because it can be seen with
certainty that it will not cause a physical change in the environment, as there are no rezonings proposed. The
proposed adoption of the 2023-2031 Housing Element is considered exempt under the “common sense”
exemption under state CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3) because the project involves policies, programs, and
actions to meet the City’s RHNA and AFFH goals that would not have any potential for causing a significant effect
on the environment, and identifies programs and actions to remove constraints and promote affordable housing
development.
OLD BUSINESS
18.
Local Roadway Safety Plan – Adoption
4 of 2271

--- page 5 ---
Adopt a Resolution to adopt the Local Roadway Safety Plan.
CEQA: The Council action is not subject to CEQA review because it can be seen with certainty that there is no
possibility that the adoption of a Local Road Safety Plan may have a significant effect on the environment as the
plan is comprised of location-based collision data and strategies to reduce collisions that may be used to develop
future safety-related projects. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15061).
19.
Council Member Compensation – Ordinance Adoption
Adopt an Ordinance increasing compensation for City Council members from $600 per month to $1,200 per
month, effective January 2025.
CEQA: This proposed ordinance is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative
activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment, and because it is a fiscal
activity that does not involve any commitment to any specific project that may result in a potentially significant
physical impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4),(5).)
REPORTS AND ANNOUNCEMENTS
City Manager, City Attorney and Council Members report on their various assignments and liaison roles and Council requests for
scheduling future items.
 
ADJOURNMENT
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
AGENDAS: Agendas and material are posted on the City’s website on the Friday preceding each Council Meeting and can be viewed on the City's
website at www.cityofsanmateo.org . Any supplemental material distributed to the Council after the posting of the agenda will be made part of the
official record.
WATCHING A MEETING ON TV: City Council meetings are broadcast live on Comcast/channel 27, Wave/channel 26, or AT&T/channel 99.
For transmission problems during the broadcast, please call (650) 522-7099. For all other broadcast comments, call (650) 522-7040, Monday-Friday,
8 a.m. - 5 p.m.
WATCHING A MEETING ON A COMPUTER: There are three ways to stream.
1) Public Meeting Portal www.cityofsanmateo.org/publicmeetings
2) City YouTube channel and stream it on YouTube: http://youtube.com/CityofSanMateo
3) Watch TV live stream: https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/193/Channel-San-Mateo-Live-Stream 
PUBLIC COMMENTS/REQUEST TO SPEAK
Prior to the Meeting 
Send comments to: clerk@cityofsanmateo.org until 4 p.m. the day of the meeting.
During the meeting
By Zoom: Click the link at the top of the agenda and you’ll be added to the meeting. All attendees are muted by default. When the item of interest
is open for consideration, select the “Raise Your Hand” icon and you will be called on at the appropriate time.
By telephone: Call (408) 638-0968 and enter the conference ID found at the top of the meeting agenda. When the item of interest is open for
consideration, select *9 to raise your hand. When called upon, press *6 to unmute, state your name and provide your comments.
In Person: At the meeting complete a “Request to Speak” form, submit a request at the speaker kiosk or scan the QR code.
ACCESSIBILITY: In compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act, those with disabilities requiring special accommodations to participate in
this meeting may contact the City Clerk’s Office at (650) 522-7040 or clerk@cityofsanmateo.org . Notification 48 hours prior to the meeting will
enable the City to make reasonable arrangements to ensure accessibility to this meeting.

CITY OF SAN MATEO

April 1, 2024
City Hall Council Chambers 5:30 PM
Special Meeting
 
CALL TO ORDER at 5:30 p.m.
Roll Call
Present:
Mayor Diaz Nash, Deputy Mayor Newsom, Council Members: Hedges, Lee, and Loraine
AB 2449 Requests and Considerations for Meeting Participation by Teleconference due to Just Cause or Emergency 
Circumstances – there were no requests. 
CLOSED SESSION
Following the opportunity for public comment, there were no speakers, the City Council convened into Closed Session at 
5:35 p.m. to consider:
1.
Conference with Legal Counsel – Anticipated Litigation
Initiation of litigation pursuant to paragraph (4) of subdivision (d) of section 54956.9: one potential case
2.
Conference with Labor Negotiators (Government Code Section 54957.6)
City designated representatives: Shawnna Maltbie, Karen Huang and Stacey Cue.
Employee Organizations: Police Sergeants’ Association, San Mateo City Employees Association, SEIU Maintenance 
and SEIU Non-Merit Part-Time
Council reconvened at 6:30 p.m.
STUDY SESSION 
3.
San Mateo Police Department Homeless Outreach Team – Presentation
Ed Barberini, Chief of Police, and Victoria Asfour and David Johnson, Homeless Outreach Coordinators, gave an 
informational presentation on the San Mateo Police Department Homeless Outreach Team.
Council asked questions of staff.
Public Comment: Four individuals provided comments that were not connected to the agenda topic. The four 
individuals were provided with warning to direct their comments to the agenda topic and permissions to speak 
were subsequently removed after failing to adhere to these warnings. 
Council praised and thanked the Homeless Outreach Team for all the excellent work they are doing.
ADJOURNMENT –The meeting adjourned at 7:13 p.m.

CALL TO ORDER at 7:30 p.m.
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll Call
Present:
Mayor Diaz Nash, Deputy Mayor Newsom, Council Members: Hedges, Lee, and Loraine
AB 2449 Requests and Considerations for Meeting Participation by Teleconference due to Just Cause or Emergency 
Circumstances – there were no requests.
CEREMONIAL
1.
National Library Week – Recognition - Sarah Meier-Heredia, Executive Director of the Library Foundation, and 
James Moore, City Librarian, gave the presentation
2.
Fair Housing Month – Proclamation - Josie Fulton, Community Outreach Coordinator, Project Sentinel, received the 
proclamation
3.
National Poetry Month – Proclamation - Lauren Lin, San Mateo County Youth Poetry Ambassador, read her poem 
and received the proclamation
CONSENT CALENDAR
The following items, 4 through 8, were considered to be routine by the City Council. Item 9 was removed to be considered 
separately. After the titles of the items were read by the Deputy City Clerk, the public was invited to comment and there 
were no speakers. Motion passed 5-0.
Moved:
Hedges, Seconded: Newsom
Ayes: 
Diaz Nash, Newsom, Hedges, Lee, and Loraine
Noes: 
None
4.
City Council Meeting Minutes - Approval
Approve the minutes of the special and regular meetings of January 16, 2024, and March 4, 2024.
5.
On-Call Traffic Engineering Data Collection Services – Amendment
Approve Amendment No. 2 to the agreement with Innovative Data Acquisitions, LLC dba IDAX Data Solutions for 
On-Call Traffic Engineering Data Collection Services to increase the agreement amount by $310,000 for a revised 
total not-to-exceed amount of $510,000; establish a contingency reserve of $30,000; and authorize the Public 
Works Director to execute the amendment in substantially the form presented and approve amendments within 
the contingency amount.

6.
Marina Lagoon Spot Dredging Project – Agreement
Approve an agreement with Moffatt & Nichol to provide professional design and professional consultant services 
for the Marina Lagoon Spot Dredging Project in the amount of $207,261; establish a contingency reserve in the 
amount of $20,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form 
presented, and execute any future amendments within the contingency amount, including no-cost term extension 
amendments.
7.
San Mateo Public Library Foundation Operating Lease – Amendment
Approve an Amendment to the Property Lease and Operating Agreement with the San Mateo Public Library 
Foundation to extend the lease for an additional year commencing on April 1, 2024, through March 31, 2025; and 
authorize its execution by the City Librarian.
8.
Boards and Commissions – Re-Establish Annual Appointment Subcommittees
Re-establish Appointment Subcommittees with Mayor Diaz Nash and the Commission Liaison to interview for 
vacancies, approve the following Council Members addition to the Appointment Subcommittees: Deputy Mayor 
Newsom to the Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission; Council Member Loraine to the Planning Commission; 
and Council Member Lee to the Park and Recreation Commission and Planning Commission, and rescind the Council 
action taken on March 18, 2024 to establish Appointment Subcommittees.
END OF CONSENT CALENDAR
REMOVED FROM CONSENT CALENDAR
9.
City Council and Boards and Commission Rules and Procedures – Update
Council Member Loraine pulled this item to clarify his comments and requested a modification.
Motion to Approve the amendments to the City Council Rules and Procedures and the Boards and Commissions 
Rules and Procedures. Motion passed 5-0.
Moved:
Hedges, Seconded: Newsom
Ayes: 
Diaz Nash, Newsom, Hedges, Lee, and Loraine
Noes: 
None
PUBLIC COMMENT
Hayley Village, Veronica, Veronica McIntyre spoke on family childcare providers. Lisa Tealer requested Council 
sponsorship of an event being organized by the Bay Area Community Health Advisory Council. Rick Sakuda spoke on 
parking on Humboldt. 
OLD BUSINESS
10.
Pedestrian Mall – Project Update/Street Barrier Selection, Business Feedback and Potential Pilot Program
Jennifer Chen, Economic Development Manager; Stephanie Piñon, Public Works Senior Engineer; and Christina 
Horrisberger, Assistant City Manager, gave the presentation.
Public Comment: Mason Fong, Matt Sweeney, Matt Sweeney, Edward Duran, Guerrero Espinoza, Rafael Salah, 
Alam Curup, Carlos Segura, Kevin Simpson, Amy Lee, Erica Wood, Robert Anderosn, Lew Cohen, Jonathan New, 
Rafat Haddad, Ronnie, Chris Homs, Akash Kapoor, Paulo Rossetto, Paulina Espizona, Drew Greenspan, an 
organized presentation led by Don Peterson, Jordan Grimes, Erika Powell, and Tanner W provided public testimony 
on the project update for the pedestrian mall.

City Council Regular Meeting Minutes – 4/1/2024
Page 3
     Council asked questions of staff and gave comments.
Council consensus was to move forward with both the pilot program and the water barriers on both blocks.
Council recessed from 9:30 p.m. to 9:40 p.m.
11.
Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Task Force Accomplishments – Update
Denise Garcia, Management Analyst II, gave the presentation.
Public Comment: Ligia Andrade Zuniga spoke to the item.
Council commented and asked questions.
Consensus of staff was for Option #2: Assign social impact and DEI initiatives to the Community Relations 
Commission.
    At 10:51 p.m. the City Council took a vote to continue the meeting beyond 11:00 p.m.  The motion carried 5-0. 
Ayes: 
Diaz Nash, Newsom, Hedges, Lee, and Loraine
Noes: 
None
PUBLIC HEARING
12.
Charter Amendments – Public Hearing
Prasanna Rasiah, City Attorney, provided a presentation on potential Charter Amendment Ballot Measures, the 
separation of different amendments, and potential timing of ballot measure.
Council commented and asked questions.
City Attorney Rasiah indicated there will be a public hearing on May 6th.
REPORTS AND ANNOUNCEMENTS
City Manager, City Attorney and Council Members reported on their various assignments and liaison roles.
Council Member Lee proposed agendizing a study session on affordable housing units administered by the City, a Council 
majority agreed to this. Council Member Lee proposed expediting a study session on historical resources program, a 
majority of Council agreed to this.  Council Member Lee asked the Council to participate in the Soul Stroll on May 15th, 
and to sponsor the Bay Area Community Health Advisory Council event with a $5,000 donation. A majority of the Council 
agreed to participate in the event. City Manager Khojikian reported he would look into the budget for this and would get 
back to the Council on this. 
ADJOURNMENT –The meeting adjourned at 11:40 p.m. 
APPROVED BY:                                                        
SUBMITTED BY:
Lisa Diaz Nash, Mayor                
Martin McTaggart, City Clerk

1.

4.
Citywide Small Trash Capture Project – Agreement
Approve an agreement with United Storm Water, Inc. for the Citywide Small Trash Capture Project in the amount of 
$697,277; establish a contingency reserve in the amount of $105,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to 
execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and issue change orders as allowed by the contract 
documents and within the contingency amount.
5.
Shoreview Park Restroom Remodel – Construction Contract
13 of 2271

--- page 14 ---
City Council Regular Meeting Minutes – 4/15/2024
Page 2
Adopt a Resolution to approve an alternative purchasing procedure to award a construction contract to Angeles 
Contractor, Inc. in the amount of $379,386.64; approve the construction contract and establish a contingency 
reserve in the amount of $57,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the contract in substantially 
the form presented and issue change orders within the contingency amount.

Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 10-6621
File ID: 24-8668
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Library Department
MEETING DATE:
May 20, 2024 
SUBJECT:
San Jose Student Intern – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve a no-cost agreement through April 26, 2029 with San Jose State University for placement of an intern and ratify 
execution of the agreement in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
The Library Department is interested in becoming an intern site for San Jose State University, School of Information. SJSU 
is the only university in the Bay Area that offers a Master’s in Library and Information Science (MLIS). It is a large program 
with plenty of eager students who want internships. The SJSU internships are unpaid but will require supervision from 
library staff as well as site insurance.
Due to the specialized nature of librarian work, it is preferred to have an intern pursuing a MLIS degree.  The intern’s 
primary focus would be assisting with the summer learning program from June to August of 2024. It has been many years 
since the library hosted an intern from SJSU, but past experiences have been positive. The intern will gain valuable 
experience that will assist them in their career, and the library would gain added capacity and support for staff. 
 
Since SJSU is a public college, the agreement is considered an intergovernmental agreement. Thus, it is required to be 
presented to City Council for approval per the City’s Authorization to Execute Contracts and Agreements policy.  The 
agreement clarifies the roles and responsibilities of the university and internship site.  However, the proposed agreement 
has already been signed, thus staff is seeking Council ratification of its execution.  
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no budgetary impact associated with this item. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not 
result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

University Organization Agreement
University-Organization Agreement
This agreement entered into on the date provided below between SAN JOSE STATE UNIVERSITY referred to as
"SJSU" and your organization (listed below), referred to as "the Learning Site".
Organization Name City of San Mateo
Date Wed, 05/22/2024
I.    STATEMENT OF PURPOSE
A.   San Jose State University
San Jose State University is committed to experiential and applied learning experiences for students. This is
accomplished through ﬁeld education, service learning, internships, research, and other forms of community
engagement that integrate the STUDENT(S)’s academic study with practical experience. Through such
activities students enhance their knowledge of their academic discipline and deepen their sense of civic
responsibility, self-awareness, and professional development.
B. Learning Site a government agency
The Learning Site is the type of organization listed above and is located at the address listed below.
C. The Learning Site and SJSU recognize the opportunity for meaningful learning experiences through
community engagement that formally integrates the student’s academic study with practical experience
within the operations of a cooperating organization. SJSU supports the goals and objectives of the Learning
Site program in which students will participate.
II.    STUDENT LEARNING
A. Program Activities
Activities will be accomplished in accordance with the student’s Learning Plan & Participation Guidelines,
reviewed and agreed upon by the STUDENT, SJSU and Learning Site prior to the start of the experience.
The STUDENT will:
Participate in all relevant trainings by the Learning Site as stated in Section III-A-2, Training and
1.
Orientation of this document.
Model professional, ethical and appropriate behavior when working with clients and when at the
2.
Learning Site.
Support the Learning Site that is a part of the student’s learning experience as speciﬁed by the Learning
3.
Plan & Participation
Guidelines.
Meet the goals of the Learning Site and the related University program in which the STUDENT is
4.
enrolled.
Fulﬁll the speciﬁc scope of work duties, identiﬁed in the student’s Learning Plan & Participation
5.
Guidelines.

program.

1.

1.
safety policies and emergency procedures and is able to act responsibly in case of an emergency.
California law may require the Learning Site to obtain ﬁngerprints of STUDENT and submit them
2.
to the Department of Justice, and/or the Federal Bureau of Investigation, for a criminal
background check. It is the Learning Site’s responsibility to: 1) Determine whether such
ﬁngerprinting is required; 2) obtain the STUDENT’S ﬁngerprints; and 3) obtain criminal
background clearance from the appropriate the Learning Site.
California law may require the Learning Site to require STUDENT to submit results of a
3.

testing is required; (2) to notify SJSU in writing of this requirement in advance of the student’s
placement at the worksite and; (3) obtain results from STUDENT.
The Learning Site representative will notify the appropriate SJSU program coordinator in writing,
4.
within 24 hours of any health & safety hazards and/or incidents of violence that occur at the
Learning Site worksite during the contract period.
SJSU will ensure that STUDENT agrees to the following:
2.

1.

as necessary and agreed upon in Section II-A, Program Activities, of this document.

termination, a student may be allowed to complete their assignments as indicated in their Learning Plan &
Participation Guidelines.

is based on STUDENT feedback, the Learning Site evaluations and SJSU faculty desire to continue this
relationship under the conditions that:

General Provisions
Indemniﬁcation
The Learning Site shall be responsible for damages caused by the negligence of its directors, agents,
employees and duly authorized volunteers occurring in the performance of this agreement. SJSU shall be
responsible for damages caused by the negligence of its directors, oﬃcers, employees and duly authorized
volunteers occurring in the performance of this agreement. It is the intention of the Learning Site and SJSU
that the provision of this paragraph be interpreted to impose on each party responsibility for the negligence of
their respective directors, oﬃcers, employees and duly authorized volunteers.
Insurance
The Learning Site shall procure and maintain General Liability insurance, commercial form, with $2,000,000
minimum limit for each Occurrence and minimum limit of $4,000,000 General Aggregate, as mutually agreed
upon for this placement agreement. This requirement can be met with a certiﬁed program of self-insurance.
The California State University system has elected to be insured for its General Liability exposure through the
self-insured CSU Risk Management Authority. The State of California has elected to be self-insured for its
vehicle liability and property exposures. As a State Learning Site, the California State University, Oﬃce of the
Chancellor, the Trustees, and the CSU system of campuses are included in this self-insured program.
Status of Student
Students shall at no time throughout this agreement be considered oﬃcers, employees, agents or volunteers
of SJSU.
Governing Law
All contracts and purchase orders shall be construed in accordance with, and their performance governed by,
the laws of the State of California. Further, the Learning Site shall comply with any state or federal law
applicable to the Learning Site’s performance under this Contract.
Assignments
Without written consent of SJSU, this agreement is not assignable by the Learning Site either in whole or in
part.
Agreement Alterations and Integration
No alteration or variation of the terms of the agreement shall be valid unless made in writing and signed by
the parties hereto, and no oral understanding or agreement not incorporated herein shall be binding on any of
the parties hereto.
22 of 2271

--- page 23 ---
Endorsement
Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as conferring on any party hereto any right to use the
other party’s name as an endorsement of product/service or to advertise, promote or otherwise market any
product or service without the prior written consent of the other parties. Furthermore, nothing in this
Agreement shall be construed as endorsement of any commercial product or service by SJSU its oﬃcers or
employees.
Survival
Upon termination of this contract for any reason, the terms, provisions, representations and warranties
contained in this agreement shall survive expiration or earlier termination of this agreement.
Severability
If any provision of this agreement is held invalid by any law, rule, order of regulation of any government, or by
the ﬁnal determination of any state or federal court, such invalidity shall not aﬀect the enforceability of any
other provision not held to be invalid.
Entire Agreement
This agreement constitutes the entire agreement and understanding of the parties with respect to the subject
matter hereof, and supersedes all prior agreements, arrangements, and understandings with respect thereto.
No representative, promise, inducement, or statement of intention has been made by any party hereto that is
not embodied herein, and no party shall be bound by or liable for any alleged representation, promise,
inducement, or statement not set forth herein.
The General Provisions are incorporated by reference and made a part of this agreement. This document
reﬂects my understanding of the relationship.
Do you agree to the conditions set forth in this UOA? Yes, I have read the University Organization
Agreement as well as the General Provisions and agree as they have been set forth.

Alex Khojikian, City Manager

May 20, 2024 
SUBJECT:
Gasoline Fuel Procurement and Delivery – Amendment 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Amendment No. 5 to the agreement with Pinnacle Petroleum, Inc. for gasoline fuel procurement and delivery to 
extend the term through June 30, 2025; increase the agreement by an amount not to exceed $515,000, for a new total 
agreement amount of $2,390,000; establish a contingency reserve in the amount of $100,000; and authorize the Public 
Works Director to execute the amendment in substantially the form presented and issue amendments within the 
contingency amount.
BACKGROUND:
The City procures the delivery of gasoline fuel to power vehicles and equipment in the Citywide fleet and maintains a 
fueling station at the Corporation Yard.  Gasoline fuel is essential for City services. 
In June 2020, a one-year service agreement was awarded by City Council to Pinnacle Petroleum, Inc. for the procurement 
and delivery of gasoline in a total not-to-exceed amount of $250,000. The approval also allowed for four (4) optional 
annual extensions of the service with annual increases to the budget and establishment of a contingency reserve.
Since the execution of the 2020 agreement, Amendments 1 through 4 were approved which exercised the annual 
extensions, added funding to the agreement, and accounted for CPI increases. Based on escalating fuel cost, the original 
five-year estimate from 2020 of $250,000 per fiscal year was inadequate. Staff reviewed the year-to-date cost and fuel use 
trends and projected a 75% annual increase. Because of this, the annual amended increases varied depending on use 
needs and market conditions. In addition, approved contingency reserves were also added annually to support any 
exceedances associated with the approved budget for each fiscal year. The current total agreement amount is $1,875,000. 
Staff recommends approval of Amendment No. 5 to extend the agreement to June 30, 2025, and increase the agreement 
by $515,000 for a new total agreement amount not to exceed $2,390,000. This Amendment No. 5 will be the last optional 
annual term extension. To address any unanticipated costs, it is recommended to establish a contingency reserve in the 
amount of $100,000.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Fuel costs are charged back to departments based on actual usage. Funding is included in the operating budgets for the 
various departments that utilize gasoline fuel and any increase in use would be absorbed in their operating budget.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
Approval of Amendment No. 5 is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a continuing administrative or maintenance 
activity involving the purchase of supplies. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(2).)

SUBJECT:
Diesel Fuel Procurement and Delivery Services – Amendment
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Amendment No. 5 to the agreement with Bosco Oil, Inc. dba Valley Oil Company for diesel fuel procurement and 
delivery to extend the term through June 30, 2025; increase the agreement amount by an amount not to exceed $310,000, 
for a new total agreement amount of $1,435,000; establish a contingency reserve in the amount of $75,000; and authorize 
the Public Works Director to execute the amendment in substantially the form presented and issue amendments within 
the contingency amount.
BACKGROUND:
The City procures the delivery of clear renewable diesel fuel to power the Citywide fleet including dump trucks, street 
sweepers, sewer flusher trucks, and light-duty maintenance trucks. Additionally, red diesel is used to power off-road 
equipment, including the Marina Lagoon Pump Station and emergency generators.
In June 2020, a one-year service agreement was awarded by City Council to Bosco Oil, Inc, dba Valley Oil Company for the 
procurement and delivery of red and clear diesel fuel in a total not-to-exceed amount of $150,000. The approval also 
allowed for four (4) optional annual extensions of the service with annual increases to the budget and establishment of a 
contingency reserve.
Since the execution of the 2020 agreement, Amendments 1 through 4 were approved and executed to provide annual 
term extensions, add funding to the agreement, and account for CPI increases. Based on escalating diesel fuel cost, the 
original five-year estimate from 2020 of $150,000 per fiscal year was inadequate. Staff reviewed the year-to-date cost and 
fuel use trends and projected a 100% annual increase in cost compared to the original estimate. Because of this, the 
annual amended increases varied depending on use needs and market conditions. In addition, approved contingency 
reserves were also added annually to support any exceedances associated with the approved budget for each fiscal year. 
The current total amended agreement amount is $1,125,000. 
Staff recommends approval of Amendment No. 5 to extend the agreement to June 30, 2025, and increase the agreement 
by $310,000 for a new total agreement amount not to exceed $1,435,000. This Amendment No. 5 will be the last optional 
annual term extension. To address any unanticipated costs, it is recommended to establish a contingency reserve in the 
amount of $75,000.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Fuel costs are charged back to departments based on actual usage. Funding is included in the operating budgets for the 
various departments that utilize diesel fuel and any increase in use would be absorbed in their operating budgets.
29 of 2271

--- page 30 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO

Approval of Amendment No. 5 is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a continuing administrative or maintenance

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue

Agenda Number:  8
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 10-4662
File ID: 24-8660
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
May 20, 2024 
SUBJECT:
Citywide Surface Treatment Packages 4A-1 and 4A-2 – Agreement 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with HDR Engineering, Inc. for design professional services for the Citywide Surface Treatment 
Packages 4A-1 and 4A-2 in an amount not to exceed $584,000; establish a contingency reserve of $58,400; and authorize 
the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and approve amendments, 
including no-cost time extensions, within the contingency amount.
BACKGROUND:
The City owns and maintains pavement assets consisting of approximately 200 miles of streets, bike paths, and alleyways. 
The Pavement Management Program (PMP) implements annual capital improvement projects focused on pavement 
maintenance beyond the routine maintenance tasks performed by the City’s field maintenance crews. 
On February 8, 2024, the City issued a Request for Proposals (RFP) for Engineering Design Services for multiple pavement 
projects including for Citywide Surface Treatment Packages 4A-1 (PMP4A1) and 4A-2 (PMP4A2) (“Projects”) that cover 
northern and southern portions of the City, respectively (see Attachments 2 and 3). These projects are for pavement 
preservation treatments only (slurry sealing and micro-surfacing). No full road-width paving or reconstruction is 
anticipated. The City received a total of six proposals that were evaluated for the proposed project team, approach, and 
estimated level of effort. 
While HDR Engineering, Inc. (HDR) was identified as the second-highest ranked proposal based on experience with similar 
projects, comprehensive approach, and favorable references, the highest-ranked firm had already been selected for a 
separate contract for the Citywide Street Rehabilitation Package 6B Project. For the PMP4A1 and PMP4A2 Projects, HDR’s 
proposed scope, schedule, and budget all align with the City’s needs. Based on the above, staff recommends entering into 
a design professional services agreement with HDR to provide design services for these Projects for an amount not to 
exceed $584,000 and establishing a contingency reserve of $58,400 (10% of the contract budget) to cover any unforeseen 
changes during the design process. The proposed term of the agreement is through December 2027.
Design of the PMP4A1 and PMP4A2 Projects is anticipated to take nine months to complete. Staff anticipates advertising 
the PMP4A1 Project for construction in March 2025, with construction beginning in May 2025. For the PMP4A2 Project, 
staff anticipates advertising for construction in September 2025, with construction beginning in April 2026 after the winter 
season. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are available in the Citywide Street Rehabilitation Package 2 (467008) project to award this agreement and 
establish the contingency reserve.

ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15301.)

This Agreement, made and entered into this day of ____________________, by and between 
the CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California 
(“CITY”), and HDR Engineering Inc., a corporation, (“CONSULTANT”), whose address is 505 14th Street, 
Suite 1110, Oakland CA 94612:
RECITALS:
A.
CITY desires certain professional engineering design services hereinafter described.
B.
CITY desires to engage CONSULTANT to provide these professional engineering design 
services by reason of its qualifications and experience for performing such services and CONSULTANT 
has offered to provide the required services on the terms and in the manner set forth herein.
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS AGREED as follows:
SECTION 1 - SCOPE OF SERVICES
The scope of services to be performed by CONSULTANT under this Agreement is as described in 
Exhibit A to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 2 - DUTIES OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for the professional quality, technical accuracy and 
coordination of all work furnished by CONSULTANT under this Agreement.  CONSULTANT shall, without 
additional compensation, correct or revise any errors or deficiencies in its work.
CONSULTANT represents that it is qualified to furnish the services described under this 
Agreement.
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for employing or engaging all persons necessary to perform 
the services of CONSULTANT.
CONSULTANT agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage ordinance as codified in 
Municipal Code Chapter 5.92, which differs from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer 
Price Index escalator.
SECTION 3 - DUTIES OF CITY
CITY shall provide pertinent information regarding its requirements for the project.

CITY shall examine documents submitted by CONSULTANT and shall render decisions pertaining 
thereto promptly, to avoid unreasonable delay in the progress of CONSULTANT's work.
SECTION 4 - TERM
The services to be performed under this Agreement shall commence upon execution of 
agreement and be completed by December 31, 2027. The term of this Agreement may be extended by 
written amendment executed by the Director of Public Works and a duly authorized representative of 
the CONSULTANT.  
SECTION 5 - PAYMENT
Payment shall be made by CITY only for services rendered and upon submission of a payment 
request upon completion and CITY approval of the work performed.  CITY shall pay all undisputed 
amounts within 30 days following receipt of CONSULTANT’s payment request. In consideration for the 
full performance of the services set forth in Exhibit A, CITY agrees to pay CONSULTANT a fee, on a time 
and expense reimbursement basis, in an amount not to exceed $584,000, pursuant to rates stated in 
Exhibit B to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 6 - TERMINATION
Without limitation to such rights or remedies as CITY shall otherwise have by law, CITY shall have 
the right to terminate this Agreement or suspend work on the Project for any reason, upon ten (10) days' 
written notice to CONSULTANT.  CONSULTANT agrees to cease all work under this Agreement upon 
receipt of said written notice.
Upon termination and upon CITY's payment of the amount required to be paid, documents 
become the property of CITY, and CONSULTANT shall transfer them to CITY upon request without 
additional compensation.
SECTION 7 - OWNERSHIP OF DOCUMENTS
All documents prepared by CONSULTANT in the performance of this Agreement, although 
instruments of professional service, are and shall be the property of CITY, whether the project for 
which they are made is executed or not. Any modification or reuse of such documents for 
purposes other than those intended by this Agreement shall be at CITY’s sole risk and without liability 
to CONSULTANT.
SECTION 8 - CONFIDENTIALITY
All reports and documents prepared by CONSULTANT in connection with the performance of 
this Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public.  CONSULTANT shall not make any 
such documents or information available to any individual or organization not employed by 
CONSULTANT or CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release.

CONSULTANT’S duty to indemnify shall survive expiration or early termination of this Agreement.

CONSULTANT shall procure and maintain for the duration of the contract and three years 
thereafter (five years for building or major improvements) the insurance specified in Exhibit C to this

SECTION 13 - NONASSIGNABILITY
Both parties hereto recognize that this Agreement is for the personal services of CONSULTANT

consent of CITY.

SKILL OF CONSULTANT
It is mutually understood and agreed by and between the parties hereto that CONSULTANT is 
skilled in the professional calling necessary to perform the work agreed to be done under this 
Agreement and that CITY relies upon the skill of CONSULTANT to do and perform the work with the 
care and skill ordinarily used by members of CONSULTANT’s profession practicing under the same or 
similar circumstances at the same locality, and CONSULTANT agrees to thus perform the work.  The 
acceptance of CONSULTANT's work by CITY does not operate as a release of CONSULTANT from said 
obligation.

SECTION 15 - WAIVERS
The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this 
Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of such 
term, covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of the same or of 
any other term, condition, ordinance, or law. The subsequent acceptance by either party of any fee or 
other money which may become due hereunder shall not be deemed to be a waiver of any preceding 
breach or violation by the other party of any term, covenant, or condition of this Agreement or of any 
applicable law or ordinance.
SECTION 16 – SEVERABILITY
If any term or portion of this Agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or otherwise 
unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall 
continue in full force and effect.
SECTION 17 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.
SECTION 18 - NON-DISCRIMINATION
CONSULTANT warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONSULTANT nor any of its 
sub-consultants shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act.
SECTION 19 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

EXHIBIT A
SCOPE OF SERVICES
PROJECT DESCRIPTION
CONSULTANT’s proposed scope of services includes evaluating the condition of the pavement of the 
street segments, conduct surveys, and recommend pavement rehabilitation solutions. CONSULTANT will 
prepare basis of design report, preliminary and final design plans, specifications and estimates for City 
to advertise the Citywide Surface Treatment Package 4A-1 and Package 4A-2 Project (Project). 
SCOPE OF WORK
TASK 1 – PROJECT MANAGEMENT AND COORDINATION
CONSULTANT will prepare a preliminary Project schedule for the City’s review. The schedule will identify 
the critical path items, review times, and milestone deliverables. CONSULTANT will update and issue the 
revised Project schedule as necessary.
Task 1.2 - Project Kickoff Meeting and Initial Site Visit
CONSULTANT (Project Manager and Staff Engineer) will coordinate and facilitate a Project kickoff 
meeting and an initial site visit with the City. The purpose of the meeting will be to gain approval of the 
proposed base schedule and to identify known Project issues. Meeting minutes will be prepared and 
distributed within five (5) working days of the meeting.
Task 1.3 - Project Review Meetings (Virtual)
CONSULTANT (Project Manager and Staff Engineer) will attend up to two (2) virtual meetings with the 
City. The primary purpose of each meeting will be to provide an overview of the milestone deliverable 
and review comments received for each milestone deliverable. A meeting will be held after the 30% 
PS&E deliverable and after the 90% PS&E deliverable for the City to provide comments on each. 
CONSULTANT will discuss comments that require clarification from the City and obtain an agreement on 
the design team’s proposed methods to address the City’s comments. CONSULTANT will provide meeting 
minutes to the City and team members. Additionally, CONSULTANT will provide the City with a Project 
progress report and an invoice monthly. 
Task 1.4 - Biweekly Meetings (Virtual)
CONSULTANT (Project Manager and Staff Engineer) will attend up to 20 virtual meetings with the City. 
The primary purpose of each meeting will be to coordinate the design efforts and resolve issues that 
arise during the design process. We will present in-progress plans to the City to facilitate discussion.
Task 1.5 - Quality Control
CONSULTANT’s designated Quality Assurance/Quality Control (QA/QC) Manager will implement the 
QA/QC procedures established for the Project. The following QC procedures will be used in the 
development of the planning and engineering documents for the Project:
•
Define key team members and their respective roles and responsibilities for the Project.
•
Identify, categorize, and incorporate correspondence, memoranda, and reports prepared for the 
Project within a distinct filing system that enables quick recovery upon request by the City.
•
Perform internal plan QC reviews by the Project Manager and task leaders to avoid conflicts and 
miscommunication.
41 of 2271

--- page 42 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 8 of 16
Rev 1/24
•
Perform QA reviews by senior management who are not involved with the development of the 
Project documents at the 30% and 90% PS&E milestones before submittal to the City.
Task 1 Deliverables
•
Baseline schedule 
•
Monthly project progress report and schedule submitted with invoice 
•
Meeting agendas and minutes 
TASK 2 – SITE INVESTIGATIONS
Task 2.1 - Field Reconnaissance and Investigation
CONSULTANT will conduct a field reconnaissance to assess the existing pavement conditions, identify 
areas of unstable subgrade (limits of dig-out repairs), and note locations of the existing utility manholes 
and valve covers. Existing ADA ramps will be field-measured, and ramp slopes will be calculated so that 
each ramp is ADA-compliant for streets requiring microsurfacing. CONSULTANT will also document 
existing curbs and gutters in need of repair.
Task 2.2 - Curb Ramp and Street Assessment Review
CONSULTANT will review the Curb Ramp Report provided by the City for each curb ramp within the street 
section improvement limits. CONSULTANT will assess each curb ramp for streets requiring 
microsurfacing and propose solutions required to meet the ADA design criteria.
Task 2.3 - Topographic Survey
The CONSULTANT Team will perform a field survey, taking survey data points at the top of curb, flow 
line, lip of gutter, and back of sidewalk at each curb ramp location for the following intersections 
anticipating curb ramp improvements associated with microsurfacing improvement only:
PMP-4A1
•
5th Avenue at El Camino Real (2 curb ramps)
•
5th Avenue at San Mateo Drive (2 curb ramps)
•
5th Avenue at Laurel Avenue (1 curb ramp)
•
5th Avenue at Ellsworth Avenue (3 curb ramps)
•
5th Avenue at B Street (1 curb ramp)
•
Alameda de Las Pulgas at Parrott Drive (3 curb ramps)
PMP-4A2
•
 • Hillsdale Boulevard at Scenic Way (1 curb ramp)
•
 • Hillsdale Boulevard at Countryside Drive (2 curb ramps)
•
 • Norfolk Street at Oxford Avenue (2 curb ramps)
The topographic survey will also include the locations of surface-visible utilities (including dips of inlets 
and maintenance holes), utility boxes, vaults, lights, signs, fire hydrants, trees and their drip lines, and 
existing driveways in the vicinity of the curb ramps.
42 of 2271

--- page 43 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 9 of 16
Rev 1/24
The survey control will be based on the California State Plane Coordinate System, North American Datum 
of 1983 (NAD 83). The vertical control will be based on the City’s vertical datum. Topographic mapping 
will be created from the survey data and will be used as the base map for the design plans.
The City’s right-of-way will be based on available City as-built drawings and County record maps (parcel 
maps, record of surveys, subdivision maps). 
Task 2.4 - Utility Investigations
CONSULTANT will conduct a search of potential utility owners (assumes up to 5 utility owners) who might 
have facilities within the Project limits. CONSULTANT will prepare utility request letters for the City to 
send to the owners, requesting record drawings of their facilities or confirming they do not have facilities 
that would be in conflict with the proposed improvements.
CONSULTANT will prepare a composite plan of existing utilities based on the information received from 
the utility owners and utility information received from the City. CONSULTANT will also identify potential 
conflicts between existing utilities and the proposed roadway improvements.
Task 2.5 – Utility Potholing (as needed)
CONSULTANT will prepare a potholing plan and discuss the proposed plan with the City. After reaching 
an agreement on the potholing plan, our potholing contractor will positively locate utilities with up to 
six (6) potholes. It is assumed that traffic control will be required and that fieldwork will be completed 
in two (2) days.
Task 2 Deliverables
•
Topographic surveys 
•
Utility pothole report 
TASK 3 – PRELIMINARY ENGINEERING
Task 3.1 – Development of Base Maps
CONSULTANT will compile the design base maps in AutoCAD, utilizing the aerial map supplemented 
with topographic survey, as needed.
Task 3.2 -30% Conceptual Plans
The 30% Conceptual Plans will include the following details:
•
Project improvement limits, curb, gutter, ADA-compliant curb ramp improvements, identification 
of potential ponding areas, and pavement rehabilitation requirements.
•
New ADA-compliant ramp layouts with identification of potential impacts to existing utilities. At 
this time, discussions will be held to determine appropriate mitigation measures that minimizes 
impacts to the Project schedule. 
•
Identification of potential construction impacts associated with the pavement improvements, 
including loss of parking, impacts to businesses and residents, lane closure requirements, and 
detour routes (as needed).
Task 3 Deliverables
•
30% design submittal including plans, specifications outline, and cost estimate 
•
Technical review workshop agenda and minutes 
43 of 2271

--- page 44 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 10 of 16
Rev 1/24
TASK 4 – PLANS, SPECIFICATIONS, AND ESTIMATES (PS&E)
Based on the concurrence of preliminary design, CONSULTANT will prepare draft PS&E packages. 
CONSULTANT will communicate with various agencies and utility companies as necessary to obtain 
information regarding the existing utilities and to identify utility conflicts. After receiving draft PS&E 
review comments from the City, CONSULTANT will prepare for and facilitate a design review workshop 
with City staff to review and resolve the City’s comments. 
CONSULTANT will prepare and submit final construction contract documents incorporating all comments 
from previous reviews. The plans will be prepared using AutoCAD and plot at appropriate scales on 22-
inch x 34-inch sheets. CONSULTANT will prepare specifications in the format of Caltrans standard 
specifications 2018 version. The City will provide City’s Standard Specification Template based on 
Caltrans 2018 for consultant’s use. All special provisions and technical specifications required for 
construction will conform to City and Caltrans specifications. CONSULTANT will assist the City in 
preparing the bid document including bid schedule and description of bid items. 
CONSULTANT will develop a schedule of bid items to address all required contract work and prepare 
clear specifications for measurement and payment. Provisions for alternate items may be considered as 
an approach to keep the Project within budget. CONSULTANT will identify any physical work required 
for project implementation outside of the construction contract and provide recommendations for the 
procurement method. 
Utility mapping and disposition will be shown on the layout plans. The disposition of existing signage 
that are impacted by the curb ramp reconstruction will be shown on the curb ramp detail plans.
The Plans anticipated to be included with each submittal are presented in the following table:

Task 4 Deliverables
•

•
Final PS&E submittals in electronic format (MS Word, Excel, and Adobe PDF) 
•
Technical review workshop agenda and minutes 
•

TASK 5 – BID SUPPORT
CONSULTANT will assist the City to respond to questions concerning the plans, specifications, and 
other bid documents prior to bid opening and prepare contract addenda, if needed. CONSULTANT will 
package the final PS&E into the construction contract bid documents and prepare electronic copies. 
CONSULTANT will be available to answer any bidders inquiries and prepare addenda as needed to 
clarify the design intent on the plans and specifications. 
Task 5 Deliverables

•
Contract addenda, if needed, for distribution by the City 
•

Project Assumptions
CONSULTANT’s proposed scope of services and budget are provided based on the following 
assumptions.
•
No change in overall roadway cross section 
•
The existing drainage systems have adequate capacity 
•
No new bicycle lanes/sidewalks are required 
•
The properties and schools will be maintained 
•
No intersection improvements other than ADA curb ramps will be included 
•
No new R/W will be required
•
Specifications will handle the project traffic control and public access requirements

EXHIBIT C
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as:
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate limit 
applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or the 
general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if CONSULTANT 
has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no less than 
$1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury 
or disease. 
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s 
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and shall 
be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT.
Other Insurance Provisions
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:
Additional Insured Status
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as additional 
insureds on the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by 
or on behalf of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or 
operations performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment 
furnished in connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the 
form of an endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 
85 or both CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used).
Primary Coverage
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.
Notice of Cancellation
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City. If a Notice 
of Cancellation endorsement is not available, CONSULTANT shall ensure the requisite Notice of 
49 of 2271

--- page 50 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 2 of 16
Rev 1/24
Cancellation is specified in the Certificate of Insurance.  In the event the Notice of Cancellation is not 
specified in the Certificate of Insurance, Consultant shall be responsible for providing the City the 
requisite notice as specified in this Exhibit C. 
Waiver of Subrogation
CONSULTANT hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said 
CONSULTANT may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  
CONSULTANT agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of 
subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of 
subrogation endorsement from the insurer.  
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the CONSULTANT to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof 
of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within 
the retention.  
Acceptability of Insurers
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City.
Verification of Coverage
CONSULTANT shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies 
of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the CONSULTANT’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  9
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 10-4662
File ID: 24-8661
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
May 20, 2024 
SUBJECT:
Citywide Street Rehabilitation Package 6B – Agreement 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with Nichols Consulting Engineers, Inc. for design professional services for the Citywide Street 
Rehabilitation Package 6B project in an amount not to exceed $820,000; establish a contingency reserve of $82,000; and 
authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and approve 
amendments, including no-cost time extensions, within the contingency amount. 
BACKGROUND:
The City owns and maintains pavement assets consisting of approximately 200 miles of streets, bike paths, and alleyways. 
The Pavement Management Program (PMP) implements annual capital improvement projects focused on pavement 
maintenance beyond the routine maintenance tasks performed by the City’s field maintenance crews. 
On February 8, 2024, the City issued a Request for Proposals (RFP) for Engineering Design Services for multiple projects 
including for the Citywide Street Rehabilitation Package 6B (PMP6B) project. This package of projects includes streets 
requiring more significant paving/reconstruction efforts.  The City received a total of six proposals, which were evaluated 
on the proposed project team, approach, and estimated level of effort. Nichols Consulting Engineers, Inc. (NCE) was 
identified as the highest-ranked proposal based on experience with similar projects, comprehensive approach, and 
favorable references. In addition, NCE’s proposed scope, schedule, and budget all align with the City’s needs. 
Based on the above, staff recommends entering a design professional services agreement with NCE to perform the design 
services for the PMP6B project in an amount not to exceed $820,000 and establish a contingency of $82,000 (10% of the 
contract budget) to cover any unforeseen changes during the design process. The proposed term of the agreement is 
through December 2026. The design of this Project is anticipated to take 12 months to complete. Staff anticipates 
advertising the project in June 2025, with construction beginning in August 2025.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are available in the Citywide Street Rehabilitation Package 2 (467008) project to award this agreement and 
establish the contingency reserve.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15301.)

AGREEMENT WITH NICHOLS CONSULTING ENGINEERS, INC.
FOR DESIGN PROFESSIONAL SERVICES
FOR
CITYWIDE SURFACE TREATEMENT PACKAGE 6-B
This Agreement, made and entered into this day of ____________________, by and between 
the CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California 
(“CITY”), and Nichols Consulting Engineers, Inc., a corporation, (“CONSULTANT”), whose address is 
1003 W. Cutting Blvd, Suite 110, Richmond CA 94804:
RECITALS:
A.
CITY desires certain professional engineering design services hereinafter described.
B.
CITY desires to engage CONSULTANT to provide these professional engineering design 
services by reason of its qualifications and experience for performing such services and CONSULTANT 
has offered to provide the required services on the terms and in the manner set forth herein.
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS AGREED as follows:
SECTION 1 - SCOPE OF SERVICES
The scope of services to be performed by CONSULTANT under this Agreement is as described in 
Exhibit A to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 2 - DUTIES OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for the professional quality, technical accuracy and 
coordination of all work furnished by CONSULTANT under this Agreement.  CONSULTANT shall, without 
additional compensation, correct or revise any errors or deficiencies in its work.
CONSULTANT represents that it is qualified to furnish the services described under this 
Agreement.
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for employing or engaging all persons necessary to perform 
the services of CONSULTANT.
CONSULTANT agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage ordinance as codified in 
Municipal Code Chapter 5.92, which differs from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer 
Price Index escalator.
SECTION 3 - DUTIES OF CITY
CITY shall provide pertinent information regarding its requirements for the project.
57 of 2271

--- page 58 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 2 of 22
Rev 1/24
CITY shall examine documents submitted by CONSULTANT and shall render decisions pertaining 
thereto promptly, to avoid unreasonable delay in the progress of CONSULTANT's work.
SECTION 4 - TERM
The services to be performed under this Agreement shall commence upon execution of 
agreement and be completed by December 31, 2026. The term of this Agreement may be extended by 
written amendment executed by the Director of Public Works and a duly authorized representative of 
the CONSULTANT.  
SECTION 5 - PAYMENT
Payment shall be made by CITY only for services rendered and upon submission of a payment 
request upon completion and CITY approval of the work performed.  In consideration for the full 
performance of the services set forth in Exhibit A, CITY agrees to pay CONSULTANT a fee, on a time and 
expense reimbursement basis, in an amount not to exceed $820,000, pursuant to rates stated in 
Exhibit B to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 6 - TERMINATION
Without limitation to such rights or remedies as CITY shall otherwise have by law, CITY shall have 
the right to terminate this Agreement or suspend work on the Project for any reason, upon ten (10) days' 
written notice to CONSULTANT.  CONSULTANT agrees to cease all work under this Agreement upon 
receipt of said written notice.
Upon termination and upon CITY's payment of the amount required to be paid, documents 
become the property of CITY, and CONSULTANT shall transfer them to CITY upon request without 
additional compensation.
SECTION 7 - OWNERSHIP OF DOCUMENTS
All documents prepared by CONSULTANT in the performance of this Agreement, although 
instruments of professional service, are and shall be the property of CITY, whether the project for 
which they are made is executed or not.
SECTION 8 - CONFIDENTIALITY
All reports and documents prepared by CONSULTANT in connection with the performance of 
this Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public.  CONSULTANT shall not make any 
such documents or information available to any individual or organization not employed by 
CONSULTANT or CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release.
58 of 2271

--- page 59 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 3 of 22
Rev 1/24
SECTION 9 - INTEREST OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT covenants that it presently has no interest, and shall not acquire any interest, 
direct or indirect, financial or otherwise, which would conflict in any manner or degree with the 
performance of the services under this Agreement.
SECTION 10 - CONSULTANT'S STATUS
It is expressly agreed that in the performance of the professional services required under this 
Agreement, CONSULTANT shall at all times be considered an independent CONSULTANT as defined in 
Labor Code Section 3353, under control of the CITY as to the result of the work but not the means by 
which the result is accomplished.  Nothing herein shall be construed to make CONSULTANT an agent or 
employee of CITY while providing services under this Agreement.
SECTION 11 - INDEMNITY
CONSULTANT agrees to hold harmless and indemnify CITY, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, loss, liability, damage, and expense arising 
out of CONSULTANT’s negligence, recklessness or willful misconduct to the maximum extent permitted 
by state law.  To the extent permitted by Civil Code section 2782.8, CONSULTANT agrees to defend 
City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents against any such claims. The 
CONSULTANT’S duty to indemnify shall survive expiration or early termination of this Agreement.
SECTION 12 - INSURANCE
CONSULTANT shall procure and maintain for the duration of the contract and three years 
thereafter (five years for building or major improvements) the insurance specified in Exhibit C to this 
Agreement.
SECTION 13 - NONASSIGNABILITY
Both parties hereto recognize that this Agreement is for the personal services of CONSULTANT 
and cannot be transferred, assigned, or subcontracted by CONSULTANT without the prior written 
consent of CITY.
SECTION 14 - RELIANCE UPON PROFESSIONAL
SKILL OF CONSULTANT
It is mutually understood and agreed by and between the parties hereto that CONSULTANT is 
skilled in the professional calling necessary to perform the work agreed to be done under this 
Agreement and that CITY relies upon the skill of CONSULTANT to do and perform the work in the most 
skillful manner, and CONSULTANT agrees to thus perform the work.  The acceptance of CONSULTANT's 
work by CITY does not operate as a release of CONSULTANT from said obligation.
