SECTION 15 - WAIVERS
The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this 
Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of such 
term, covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of the same or of 
any other term, condition, ordinance, or law. The subsequent acceptance by either party of any fee or 
other money which may become due hereunder shall not be deemed to be a waiver of any preceding 
breach or violation by the other party of any term, covenant, or condition of this Agreement or of any 
applicable law or ordinance.
SECTION 16 – SEVERABILITY
If any term or portion of this Agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or otherwise 
unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall 
continue in full force and effect.
SECTION 17 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.
SECTION 18 - NON-DISCRIMINATION
CONSULTANT warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONSULTANT nor any of its 
sub-consultants shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act.
SECTION 19 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

(Project). The following streets and limits are included in this scope of services:

SCOPE OF WORK
TASK 1 – PROJECT MANAGEMENT AND MEETINGS 
1A) Project Kick-off Meeting / Progress Meetings 
CONSULTANT will arrange a kick-off meeting with the City to initiate work on the project. The objectives 
will be:
•
Review of the Scope of Work 
•
Establish Lines of Communication
•
Establish Project Schedule and Milestones 
•
Define Design and Operation Criteria 
It is critical to establish effective lines of communication with, and coordination amongst, the various 
stakeholders from the start to ensure the delivery of a high-quality project within budget and on 
schedule. In addition to City staff (Engineering, Maintenance, etc.), CONSULTANT will research and 
coordinate, as needed, with other agencies such as PG&E, AT&T, Caltrans, California Water Service and 
Westborough Water District, San Mateo Sewer, Stormwater and Wastewater, Schools, etc., to identify 
any potential conflicts, requirements, or design issues early to help minimize delays (and costs) later in 
the design process or during construction. At the Kick-Off Meeting, key deliverables for each Task and 
the Project Schedule would be reviewed and adjusted accordingly to meet City needs.
Throughout the project, CONSULTANT will be available to attend bi-weekly progress meetings with the 
City to maintain frequent communication and efficient decision making with the City. Bi-weekly meeting 
summaries will be prepared and submitted to the City that include completed activities summary, 
scheduled activities for next month, any issues identified from field review or data collection, updated 
project schedule, budget status, and other important project information identified in the kick-off 
meeting. The purpose of the progress meetings will be to identify and resolve any design or funding 
issues in a timely manner, present design alternatives and recommendations to City staff, and continue 
coordination with project stakeholders as necessary. Specific design submittal review meetings with City 
staff will be scheduled at each submittal milestone.
Task 1A Deliverables: Kick-off/Progress Meeting Agendas and Summaries
1B) Utility Coordination 
As noted in the project Key Assumptions, CONSULTANT will share notification letters to applicable utility 
providers with a preliminary project schedule for street maintenance and rehabilitation, followed by 
planned treatment or excavation depth and approximate utility location information. This notification 
will occur at the time of project kick-off to allow utility providers to perform maintenance on their 
facilities prior to utility cut moratorium on the street paving areas and evaluate need for utility 
64 of 2271

--- page 65 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 9 of 22
Rev 1/24
adjustments/lowering. 
CONSULTANT will document all utility coordination notifications, conversations, and meetings with 
utility contacts and information in a matrix format with dates of correspondence. CONSULTANT will also 
keep the City informed of any project delays related to utilities. 
Task 1B Deliverables: Utility notification letters and utility contact matrix. 
TASK 2 – PAVEMENT REHABILITATION DESIGN 
2A) Pavement and C&G Condition Survey 
CONSULTANT will perform a visual pavement condition survey of each street section based on distresses. 
The surveys will generally cover the travel and parking lanes and serve the purpose of refining the 
appropriate pavement treatments and identifying any potential problematic subgrade or drainage 
issues.
The survey will note the presence of load related and environmental distresses as they pertain to 
developing appropriate pavement treatments. Potential base repairs will be identified, marked, and 
recorded using GPS based software. CONSULTANT scope of work and condition surveys do not address 
issues including but not limited to traffic, safety and road hazards, geometric issues, or short-term 
maintenance that should be performed (i.e., pothole that should be repaired).
A visual assessment of all C&G will be included to identify areas where drainage or the adjacent 
pavement has been compromised. Based on criteria established with the City, CONSULTANT will 
determine what level of curb and gutter replacement will be pursued for design plans and documents. 
If damaged C&G areas are more extensive than anticipated, it may require additional time to complete 
the surveys and CONSULTANT will notify the City. A summary table of repair candidates will be prepared 
with pertinent information including street, location, length, and picture, for City review and 
confirmation. 
Task 2A Deliverables: Summary table of curb and gutter repair candidates. 
2B) Pavement Coring 
CONSULTANT will collect pavement section core samples (4-inch to 8-inch diameter cores) on all 
rehabilitation and potential reconstruction street sections at locations determined by CONSULTANT. 
CONSULTANT current fee estimate assumes up to 73 core locations that CONSULTANT estimates will 
take up to 10 days to complete. For each core sample, CONSULTANT will measure and record the 
thickness of asphalt and concrete encountered in the pavement structural section, including the 
presence of any pavement reinforcing fabric. 
Bulk samples of subgrade will be obtained in support of potential street reconstruction in accordance 
with the Key Assumptions. Up to 21 bulk samples (at least one bulk sample on each pavement section 
for rehabilitation) of subgrade materials will be tested for R-value, moisture content and Atterberg Limits 
(plasticity index, PI). The AB thickness will be measured/estimated in accordance with the Key 
Assumptions. CONSULTANT will arrange for traffic control services during pavement coring, which will 
conform to the latest standards contained within the MUTCD Part VI and the Caltrans Traffic Manual 
Chapter 5 “Traffic Control for Construction and Maintenance Work Zones,” as well as any local 
requirements that exist. CONSULTANT will also obtain an encroachment permit for pavement coring if 
required. CONSULTANT will also develop a map showing proposed coring locations for the City to review 
and to facilitate the encroachment permit if required.
65 of 2271

--- page 66 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 10 of 22
Rev 1/24
Task 2B Deliverables: Map of proposed coring locations. 
2C) Pavement Deflection Testing 
CONSULTANT will perform non-destructive pavement deflection testing in accordance with California 
Test Method (CTM) 356 to evaluate the structural capacity of the existing pavement section on the 
subject arterial and collector streets to be rehabilitated. The FWD is a conceptually simple yet powerful 
device for measuring the response of pavements under simulated wheel loads and estimating their 
structural capacities. All travel lanes of each arterial and collector street section will be tested at 
approximately 100-foot intervals. 
Approximately 260 data points will be collected and CONSULTANT estimates that the FWD testing will 
take 1.5 days to complete. CONSULTANT will also include deflection testing in encroachment permits 
referenced under Key Assumptions, similar to coring work. Additionally, CONSULTANT will provide traffic 
control in accordance with the same standards discussed in pavement coring task.
2D) Pavement Design Recommendations 
Using the data obtained in Task 2, CONSULTANT will perform pavement analysis and design services, and 
develop pavement maintenance, rehabilitation, and reconstruction recommendations for each street 
section. CONSULTANT will perform its analysis in accordance with the Caltrans Highway Design Manual 
and develop pavement structural section recommendations expressed in the form of a Traffic Index (TI) 
that will be provided by the City. CONSULTANT will develop recommendations including, but not limited 
to, the following:
•
Surface Seal Applications: Slurry seal, Microsurfacing (Type II and Type III), Rubberized chip seal, 
combinations thereof
•
Rehabilitation: Mill and Overlay, Cold-in-Place Recycling (CIR), Rubberized Hot Mix Asphalt 
(RHMA) 
•
Reconstruction (as needed): Hot Mix Asphalt (HMA) over AB, Full-Depth HMA, and FDR
•
Locations and treatments of failed pavement sections (base repairs)
CONSULTANT will then summarize its recommendations in a pavement design memorandum to the City 
that, at a minimum, will include the following:
•
Results of pavement condition surveys, deflection testing, coring, and laboratory testing
•
Description of testing procedures and analysis performed for the project
•
Recommended alternatives for rehabilitation and preventive maintenance
CONSULTANT will submit an electric copy of its draft technical and basis of pavement design 
memorandum to the City for initial review. Preliminary design results will be presented to the City 
including facilitating a workshop that will be attended by the Public Works Director and Deputy Director 
of Public Work for acceptance prior to proceeding with detailed design work. Upon receipt of any 
comments from the City, CONSULTANT will then prepare its final technical and basis of pavement design 
memorandum, which will be signed and stamped by CONSULTANT. The final technical and basis of design 
pavement memorandum will then be provided to the City reflecting any comments on the draft technical 
66 of 2271

--- page 67 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 11 of 22
Rev 1/24
memorandum. CONSULTANT staff, including the project manager, lead civil project engineer, and 
principal engineers will also be available for up to two (2) additional strategy meetings to discuss 
pavement treatments, subgrade issues, costs, geometric considerations (e.g. drainage and cross-slopes) 
pavement performance and longevity, construction impacts, and utility constraints.
Task 2 Deliverables: Draft/final pavement technical and basis of pavement design memorandums, 
technical review workshop agenda and meeting summary, meeting agendas and summaries for up to 
two (2) strategy meetings.
TASK 3 – PLANS, SPECIFICATIONS & ESTIMATES (PS&E)  
3A) Design Data Gathering 
CONSULTANT will review relevant available data and records from the City as listed in Task 1 and stated 
Key Assumptions, and other sources that may be appropriate to support the preparation of project 
contract documents. These may include, but are not limited to, as-built street improvement and 
infrastructure plans, including any preliminary plans for future work that may conflict with this project. 
The gathered information will be compiled and included in the base map used for design. 
Available record data (e.g., utilities, right-of-way) used to prepare street base maps will account for utility 
lids or surface features within the roadway which may be affected by paving operations. CONSULTANT 
will conduct a field survey, using GPS based software to collect existing roadway features including 
roadway geometry from visual inspection, utility covers, typical cross-slopes at pedestrian crossings, 
striping and markings, and relevant traffic signage.    
Record data for streets to have topographic survey will be shown in their approximate location. Utility 
depths from the GPR survey (Optional Task) and available as-builts will be included in the base map for 
streets to be reconstructed. Based on CONSULTANT review CONSULTANT will identify any data gaps or 
missing information and provide this information to the City for review and discussion. CONSULTANT 
will discuss with the City whether rehabilitation work must occur in the right-of-way, in order to save the 
City the cost and fees associated with obtaining encroachment permits, if at all possible. 
Task 3A Deliverables: As needed encroachment permits, base maps (included in subsequent tasks)
3B) Topographic Survey 
CONSULTANT’s Project Surveyor will complete monument/control recovery, field investigation and field 
surveys sufficient to prepare a topographic mapping product for select street locations and proposed 
curb ramp design locations, as stated under Key Assumptions. The mapping shall be compiled at an 
agreed upon scale with a 1’ contour interval and will be based on the City Datum. 
The mapping area for Alameda de las Pulgas shall be a corridor along the street section between the 
existing back of walk to opposite back of walk, starting and stopping at the designated locations and 
including through the returns of the terminating streets. In the absence of an existing sidewalk, the 
survey will extend to 5 feet behind the curb or edge of pavement, or up to the first substantial barrier 
(wall, fence, etc.). Mapping will extend 25 feet beyond the curb return up each intersecting side street. 
The topographic data to be collected is more specifically defined as:
•
Cross-sections at 50’ intervals along with detailed surface topography and/or additional spot 
elevations as required defining the longitudinal and cross-slope grade breaks. 
67 of 2271

--- page 68 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 12 of 22
Rev 1/24
•
Surveyed locations for significant surface features. A structure inventory for sanitary and 
storm drain facilities reflecting rim and invert elevations, pipe direction and size will be 
provided for accessible structures. 
•
Location of all surface evident street monumentation within the mapping corridor.
•
Location of underground utility locator markings, where provided.
•
Provide a finished draft topographic survey for each area of work and AutoCAD Civil3D DTM 
surface for each street section.
CONSULTANT’s surveyor will calculate the record data location of the existing right-of-way for the 
mapping corridor and will calculate and show the location of each adjoining parcel lot line from record 
data (assessor’s parcel data, record mapping and apparent lines of occupation). It should be noted that 
property line data will not be based upon a field survey and is considered approximate.
Survey on N. Humboldt Street will extend up to the street centerline and that at 805 Highland Avenue 
will focus primarily on the curb and gutter.  
Task 3B Deliverables: Topographic survey results.
3C) Preliminary Design (30% P&E) 
Using data collected in previous tasks, CONSULTANT will develop preliminary design documents (30% 
level of completion) for each street section. Preliminary design drawings will be based on base maps 
prepared using information collected in previous tasks and as stated in Key Assumption. Drawings will 
include existing right-of-way and depict basic street layout and preliminary pavement treatments with 
pertinent geometric information. Drawings will be scaled at a scale of 1-inch = 20-feet.
The Engineer’s Cost Estimate will be prepared in MS Excel format and based on the most recent 
construction cost data available to CONSULTANT for projects of this type.  The 30% plans will be prepared 
in AutoCAD format on 22-inches x 34-inches sheets. It is assumed that the City will require a 15-day 
review/comment period once each 30% P&E package is submitted.
In combination with CONSULTANT’s pavement design and basis of design technical memorandums, 
during development of the 30% P&E CONSULTANT will discuss and confirm pavement treatment 
alternatives, subgrade stabilization measures, street geometric constraints and cross-slopes, curb and 
gutter replacement, non-compliant curb ramps, drainage issues, and summary of utility location data 
and possible utility conflicts/concerns. CONSULTANT’s staff including the project manager, lead civil 
project engineer, and principal engineers will also be available for up to two (2) additional strategy 
meetings during preliminary design for each design package. Strategy discussions will focus on key design 
elements and constraints, overall schedule and budget, community outreach and key issues, lessons 
learned from previous projects and how to structure and develop construction documents with respect 
to reducing construction impacts, bidding strategies including packaging and alternates, sequencing of 
streets and work, and contingencies. 
Task 3C Deliverables: Meeting agendas and summaries for up to two (2) strategy meetings per design 
package. One reproducible copy of 30% plans, engineer’s estimate, and summary of agreed upon design 
criteria and decisions for each design package.
68 of 2271

--- page 69 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 13 of 22
Rev 1/24
3D) 90% PS&E 
Preliminary P&E (30% P&E) will be revised to incorporate comments received from the City as well as 
agreed upon design criteria and decisions, from which the 90% PS&E will be prepared. CONSULTANT will 
provide a response to each comment that is included in a comment table provided by the City. 
The 90% plans will incorporate typical design elements, existing and proposed contours (where 
available), pavement treatments, right-of-way parcel lines, wedge or conform grind locations, typical 
cross-sections, C&G repairs, gutter flow line for drainage repairs, curb ramp replacement and details and 
limits of work, and site-specific striping and signage plans. Plan sheets will include base repair schedules 
and show individual repair locations. 
CONSULTANT will prepare the project technical Special Provisions in MS Word format and follow the 
City’s formatting conventions, with reference to Caltrans 2018 Standard Specifications and City standard 
provisions. CONSULTANT may recommend deviating from Caltrans standards where, from 
CONSULTANT’s experience, such changes will improve the likelihood of achieving a successful 
construction project for a local agency without compromising the integrity of the design. 
The Engineer’s Cost Estimate will be updated to reflect the refined construction design. It is assumed 
that the City will require a 15-day review/comment period once each 90% PS&E package is submitted.
Task 3D Deliverables: One reproducible copy of 90% plans, technical specifications, and engineer’s 
estimate
3E) Final (Bid Set) PS&E 
The 90% PS&E will be revised to incorporate comments received from the City to prepare the Final (Bid 
Set) PS&E. Similar to 90% PS&E, CONSULTANT will provide a response to each comment that is included 
in a comment table provided by the City. The Final (Bid Set) PS&E will include all notes and details 
necessary for construction. One electronic copy of the final (Bid Set) PS&E will then be packaged and 
submitted similar to the 90% PS&E unless directed otherwise. Upon receipt of the City’s final review 
comments, the project documents will be finalized for bidding purposes. A final quantity calculation will 
be tabulated and entered into the final Engineer’s Cost Estimate for each project. All final documents 
will be reviewed, stamped, and signed by CONSULTANT’s registered civil engineer, and the Final PS&E 
will be delivered to the City in electronic format.
Task 3E Deliverables: One electronic file of the final plans, technical specifications, and engineer’s 
estimate for each design package. The electronic files for the final construction plans, specifications, and 
engineer’s estimate will be in AutoCAD 2022 or later version, Microsoft Word, and Microsoft Excel, 
respectively.
TASK 4 – BIDDING SUPPORT SERVICES 
CONSULTANT will provide the City with assistance during the advertisement and bidding periods of each 
project including attending the pre-bid conference, responding to questions about the project design, 
and preparation of any addenda and/or clarifications to the PS&E that are deemed necessary. 
CONSULTANT has included up to 40 hours of staff time for bidding support. Any effort beyond that will 
be on a time and materials basis at an additional cost. 
Task 4 Deliverables: Attend pre-bid meetings; Prepare responses to questions received regarding project 
design; Prepare bid addenda as necessary; and assist with bid responsiveness (as needed).
69 of 2271

--- page 70 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 14 of 22
Rev 1/24
TASK 5 – OPTIONAL TASKS
5A) Construction Support Services 
CONSULTANT will provide up to 132 hours of support services to the City during construction. At a 
minimum, these services are anticipated to include attendance at the pre-construction conference, 
reviewing contractor submittals and responding to contractor RFIs, field verification of localized repair 
(base repairs and C&G) areas, subgrade repairs after proof rolling, curb ramp layouts, providing 
recommendations for any necessary construction changes due to unforeseen field conditions, and 
reviewing construction for acceptance. This task does not provide any field inspection services. If effort 
beyond the estimated hours is necessary, CONSULTANT staff time will be delivered on a time and 
materials basis at an additional cost.
Task 5A Deliverables: Attend Pre-Construction Conference; Assist with review of contractor submittals 
and RFIs as necessary; Provide recommendations for any necessary construction changes due to 
unforeseen conditions; and Assist with review of contract change orders.
5B) Utility Location 
Using GPR, CONSULTANT’s utility locator will field locate utility alignments and depths for utility mains 
and laterals for utilities within all streets identified for reconstruction in accordance with Key 
Assumptions to the extent that GPR methods can detect utilities. GPR along utility mains will be marked 
at various locations along each street section, and the accuracy for the electronic depths will depend on 
the soil conditions and utility material. Traffic control will consist of a 1-man crew with traffic control 
arrow board and signs. Pressure washing of paint marks is excluded from this scope.
Task 5B Deliverables: Marked utility depth and alignments and electronic collection of utility information.
5C) Utility Potholing  
If GPR cannot establish utility alignments and or depths at some locations, potholing may need to be 
performed to identify necessary utility relocations or adjustments as well as validation of sufficient 
clearance. CONSULTANT assumed up to 20 potholes will be conducted to verify underground utility 
depth in accordance with stated assumptions. CONSULTANT will discuss with the City to confirm selected 
utility locations for optional potholing in this project. 
Task 5C Deliverables: Updated utility information to be summarized in the pothole report.
SCOPE KEY ASSUMPTIONS 
City’s Responsibilities
CONSULTANT has assumed that the City will be able to provide the following to the extent available:
1. Any information readily available regarding utilities (i.e., water, sanitary sewer, electrical, and 
storm drain), boundary and property limits, tree locations, site features, and existing or planned 
construction.
2. Readily available aerial or topographic maps.
3. Project requirements including design objectives, budget, constraints, and criteria.
70 of 2271

--- page 71 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 15 of 22
Rev 1/24
4. Existing City technical specifications as the basis for developing/modifying new technical 
specifications.
5. Front-End specifications, including General Provisions and Special Provisions.
6. City Standard Provisions and Standard Design Criteria or details.
Pavement Testing and Design
1. Deflection testing will be conducted on arterial and collector street sections currently identified 
for rehabilitation for the PMP-6B project. Up to 1.5 days of deflection testing are assumed.
2. Pavement coring will be performed with spacing criteria and bulk samples as follows:
a. Up to 73 pavement cores will be obtained. Coring frequency is approximately every 500 
lane feet for potential reconstruction, 1,000 lane feet for rehabilitation, and 1,500 lane 
feet for surface seal candidate street which are borderline candidate for rehabilitation, 
with a minimum of two cores per street section and cores taken in shoulder or parking 
lanes where a street is crowned. 
b. A minimum of two (2) bulk samples of subgrade will be obtained per rehabilitation street 
section for laboratory testing including R-value, Plasticity Index and Moisture Content. 
Bulk samples will not be collected for surface seal street sections. 
c.
Aggregate base (AB) thickness will be measured at all bulk sample locations and will be 
measured or estimated in thickness at remaining pavement coring locations using 
dynamic cone penetrometer (DCP).
d. As requested by the City, pavement core holes will be backfilled with base material and 
non-shrink cement grout.
3. Base repairs will be measured in length and width to estimate base repair bid quantities for all 
street sections. It is the intent that base repair quantities will be for bidding quantity purposes 
only and that actual locations will be marked by CONSULTANT with the City’s inspectors prior to 
construction.
4. A no-fee encroachment permit will be obtained with the City for all pavement testing, if required.
Topographic Surveying and Base Maps
1. As requested by the City, a topographic and boundary survey (where applicable) will be 
completed for the following street locations:
a. Alameda de las Pulgas, from 39th Avenue to La Casa Avenue (1,900 LF).
b. 805 Highland Avenue, for 100 linear feet at location of tree root uplift (100 LF).
c.
East side of N. Humboldt Street, from Bayswater Avenue to Howard Avenue (630 LF). 
2. Except for those noted above, all streets will utilize County parcel/aerial base maps, as streets 
are assumed to not include significant drainage improvements, complex changes in street 
geometry or intersection improvements, or full pavement section reconstruction. Street designs 
71 of 2271

--- page 72 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 16 of 22
Rev 1/24
will be drawn to 20-scale. Should CONSULTANT determine that topographic information is 
required, CONSULTANT can provide topographic survey work for additional scope and fee.
3. As requested by the City, a topographic survey will be completed at all proposed curb ramp 
design locations. The survey will extend 25 feet on either side of the ramp location. 
Approximately 113 curb ramp locations are assumed to be surveyed and designed. Boundary 
survey is not included at these survey locations. The mapping will be based upon assumed 
horizontal and vertical datums, and durable survey control points will be established for later use 
during the construction phase. 
4. Assume all street improvement work will be within City right-of-way, as this has impacts to the 
level of effort required to completion of design.
Utility Coordination and Location
1. CONSULTANT will prepare and distribute utility notification letters to collect facility maps and as-
builts, confirm utility planned work, and notify utilities of the City’s utility cut moratorium on 
improved streets. CONSULTANT will prepare and distribute a second round of letters including 
utility location information based on GPR (Optional Task) that is provided for street sections 
proposed for reconstruction to solicit feedback and need for utility relocation or adjustments.
2. Utility field location will be completed using GPR (Optional Task) to field locate and mark utility 
alignments and approximate depths of mains and laterals within street sections which are 
candidates for reconstruction (e.g., FDR) to the extent that GPR can detect utilities. Utility 
location markings will be included in topographic surveys. GPR data is for planning purposes and 
will be provided as a courtesy to utilities to help expedite their assessments. Utilities will be 
responsible to review their facilities, perform additional utility potholes, if necessary, to identify 
any conflict which require adjustment of their facilities.
3. Underground utility locating will not be performed for surface seal or rehabilitation streets. 
However, utility covers will be inventoried and shown and/or quantified on plans to develop bid 
quantities for adjusting and/or protection of utility covers.
4. If pavement reconstruction is required instead of rehabilitation, CONSULTANT will discuss with 
the City utility location options (e.g., GPR) and can provide for additional scope and fee for those 
additional sections.
5. If required, CONSULTANT has included up to 20 utility potholing locations as an optional task that 
are assumed to be patched with cold patch in the event there are key locations where critical 
utility facilities require greater accuracy or verification. Otherwise, utility locations will be 
identified from GPR survey results for reconstruction streets.
Traffic Striping
1. Traffic striping is assumed to match existing patterns and include adjustments to meet current 
CA Manual of Uniform Traffic Control Devices (MUTCD) standards.
72 of 2271

--- page 73 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 17 of 22
Rev 1/24
2. Up to an additional 5 signage and striping sheets are assumed to be prepared to implement 
bicycle and/or pedestrian improvements which are noted in the City’s Bicycle Master Plan and 
Transit Oriented Development Pedestrian Access Plan.
3. No significant traffic striping changes or design requiring traffic studies or assessment of traffic 
impacts is planned or included as a part of this scope.
4. Striping plans will only be developed for all street sections. Striping types and quantities will be 
inventoried for the purposes of developing bid quantities.
Traffic Control Plans
1. Traffic control plans are not included in the scope of work. It is assumed the City’s contractor will 
prepare traffic control plans in accordance with project technical specifications and requirements 
and submit to the City for review.
Curb Ramps, Crosswalks, and Sidewalk
1. It is assumed that non-compliant curb ramps will be replaced or retrofitted (excluding slurry seal 
streets) in accordance with Caltrans standards. Curb ramps will be replaced or retrofitted based 
on guidance from the DOJ/DOT Joint Technical Assistance on the Title II of the Americans with 
Disabilities Act Requirements to Provide Curb Ramps when Streets, Roads, or Highways are 
Altered through Resurfacing dated July 8, 2013. Further clarification on curb ramp replacement 
included in this scope of work is as follows:
a. Utility relocations (e.g., utility poles, utility boxes, and drainage inlets) required to 
construct curb ramps are not included in this scope of work. 
b. All ramp construction shall be within City R/W. Where available County parcel information 
is to be used, City R/W is assumed to be at existing back of walk. 
c.
For curb ramps within hills or steepened grades, curb ramp design shall only extend up to 
15 feet in the uphill direction, beyond which is considering chasing the grade.
2. Crosswalks and implied walkways between curb ramps will be designed in accordance with the 
latest Public Right-of-Way Accessibility Guidelines (PROWAG) and Caltrans Design Information 
Bulletin (DIB) 82-06 accessibility guidance for both running slope and cross-slope. This guidance 
will be reviewed with the City to confirm design approach.
3. Site-specific curb ramp details will be provided and use collected topographic survey information.
4. Sidewalk repair survey and replacement is not included in this scope of work but can be added if 
required for additional scope and fee. 
Stormwater Drainage and Compliance
1. The project will, to the extent possible, and within the context of proposed rehabilitation and 
preventive maintenance, address visible drainage issues. No major drainage improvements are 
assumed for this project requiring significant stormwater drain and pipe alteration and/or 
reconstruction.
73 of 2271

--- page 74 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 18 of 22
Rev 1/24
2. As needed, the project will manage drainage patterns based on geometric changes to street 
grades and widths for proposed improvements requiring new flow lines and replacement of 
storm drains as needed.
3. This project will be designed in accordance with the recent third reissuance of the San Francisco 
Bay Municipal Regional Stormwater Permit (MRP 3.0) and the new regulated project thresholds 
in Provision C.3 of the SMWPPP. Although not currently anticipated, streets which will be 
reconstructed and the contiguous 1-acre threshold is exceeded, additional scope and fee for the 
design of green infrastructure may be provided.
Encroachment Permit
1. If the limits of pavement improvements are found to extend into a neighboring City, 
CONSULTANT will complete and obtain the required City encroachment permit for construction, 
if needed, with permitting fees paid by the City.
2. Based on the City’s project street lists, multiple project street sections are adjacent to El Camino 
Real (Caltrans R/W). The City has indicated that it will prepare Caltrans encroachment permit 
applications, as needed.   
3. CONSULTANT will apply for no-fee City encroachment permits for all field work, including 
pavement coring and falling weight deflectometer (FWD), if required.

EXHIBIT C
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as:
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate limit 
applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or the 
general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if CONSULTANT 
has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no less than 
$1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury 
or disease. 
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s 
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and shall 
be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT.
Other Insurance Provisions
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:
Additional Insured Status
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used).
Primary Coverage
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.
Notice of Cancellation
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City. If a Notice 
of Cancellation endorsement is not available, CONSULTANT shall ensure the requisite Notice of 
77 of 2271

--- page 78 ---
Design Professional Services 2024
Page 2 of 22
Rev 1/24
Cancellation is specified in the Certificate of Insurance.  In the event the Notice of Cancellation is not 
specified in the Certificate of Insurance, Consultant shall be responsible for providing the City the 
requisite notice as specified in this Exhibit C. 
Waiver of Subrogation
CONSULTANT hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said 
CONSULTANT may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  
CONSULTANT agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of 
subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of 
subrogation endorsement from the insurer.  
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the CONSULTANT to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof 
of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within 
the retention.  
Acceptability of Insurers
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City.
Verification of Coverage
CONSULTANT shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies 
of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the CONSULTANT’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

SUBJECT:
Train Horn Noise Quiet Zone Projects – Service Agreement 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve a Service Agreement with the Peninsula Corridor Joint Powers Board for the Train Horn Noise Quiet Zone projects 
at Villa Terrace and East Bellevue Avenue in the amount of $59,440; establish a contingency reserve in the amount of 
$9,000; and authorize the Director of Public Works to execute the Agreement in substantially the form presented and 
approve amendments within the contingency amount. 
BACKGROUND:
On June 20, 2023, at the Special City Council Meeting, staff presented an update to the Citywide Train Horn Noise Quiet 
Zone (Quiet Zone) effort, which would minimize train horn noise near rail crossings. To establish a Quiet Zone under 
Federal Railway Administration requirements, staff proposed the following projects, which were recommended by a 2022 
Traffic Study as the most cost-effective option with minimal impacts on residents and businesses (Improvements):
•
Villa Terrace: The permanent closure of the Villa Terrace at-grade railroad crossing. Pedestrians, bicyclists, and 
motorists will no longer be able to cross the railroad tracks at this location. 
•
East Bellevue Avenue: Installation 100-foot traffic safety medians near the railroad crossing on both sides of 
the tracks. 
On February 19, 2019, Council authorized Public Works to enter into agreements with three firms, including Bellecci & 
Associates, Inc. (Bellecci) for on-call professional consulting services for various capital improvement projects.  In January 
2024, after soliciting proposals from all three on-call consultants, the City selected Bellecci for design and project 
management support of the Improvements. Bellecci has now completed preliminary design and is progressing to detailed 
design while considering feedback from public outreach and stakeholders wherever feasible.
To educate the community about the Improvements, in early 2024 staff performed comprehensive community outreach, 
including bilingual outreach materials (e.g. approximately 3,000 mailed notices, E-news updates, and physical posters at 
the proposed project sites), coordination with neighbourhood schools and property management companies, City social 
media updates, door-knocking at over 300 homes, and an online community survey. Staff will continue outreach to ensure 
the Improvements best align with the community’s needs.
Coordination with Peninsula Corridor Joint Powers Board (PCJPB)
The Improvements occur adjacent to and within PCJPB Right-of-Way. The proposed Service Agreement (Attachment 2) is 
required for PCJPB review of the design of the Improvements in accordance with PCJPB design and safety standards, and 
overall coordination of the Improvements. PCJPB estimated an Agreement amount of $59,440. Staff recommends a 
contingency amount of $9,000 (15% of the agreement’s budget) to cover unforeseen costs, as needed. Any unused funds 
81 of 2271

--- page 82 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
will be returned to the City, and PCJPB will not charge more than the Agreement amount unless an Amendment to the 
proposed Service Agreement is approved. In addition, as the design is nearing completion, PCJPB and the City plan to 
amend this Agreement in the future to include PCJPB’s fees during construction. If such fees exceed the contingency 
amount, staff will bring this item back to City Council with a recommendation to approve a subsequent Amendment.
Next Steps
The design of these Improvements is anticipated to be complete in late Fall 2024. Staff anticipates advertising the project 
for construction by the end of 2024. After construction is complete, staff will apply for Quiet Zone status by end of 2025. 
Staff will present an update for the Quiet Zone effort at a City Council Study Session on June 17, 2024, and bring a future 
construction contract award recommendation to City Council following the completion of design. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are available in the Train Horn Noise Project (462205) to fund this agreement.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
Approval of a Service Agreement between the City and the PCJPB is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen 
with certainty that there is no possibility that the review of the Improvements design intended to minimize train horn 
noise may have a significant effect on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Train Horn Noise Quiet Zone - Villa Terrace Closure; East Bellevue Avenue Medians
To establish a Train Horn Noise Quiet Zone, the City must establish Federal Railway Administration (FRA) safety measures. The proposed safety measures are the closure of Villa Terrace at the
railroad tracks, and installation of medians on E Bellevue Avenue.

SUBJECT:
Bermuda Drive Utility Relocation Project – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve an agreement with Pacific Gas & Electric for the Bermuda Drive Utility Relocation Project in 
the amount of $186,685.48; and authorize the Director of Public Works to execute the Agreement in substantially the form 
presented. 
BACKGROUND:
On May 21, 2018, City Council approved the award of a professional services agreement with Biggs Cardosa Associates 
(BCA) for design and environmental documentation services for the Bermuda Drive Bridge Replacement Project. During 
design, BCA identified a utility conflict that will impede construction of the bridge replacement. On the north side of the 
bridge, existing overhead Pacific Gas & Electric (PG&E) electrical lines prohibit pile installation equipment to be raised and 
install the required bridge piles. These overhead lines need to be placed underground and out of the way of the pile 
equipment prior to the bridge construction work. The Bermuda Drive Utility Relocation Project (Project) is a subproject of 
the larger Bermuda Drive Bridge Replacement Project and will underground these electrical lines. 
The Project is a joint effort by the City and PG&E. On December 4, 2023, City Council approved award of a construction 
agreement with St. Francis Electric to perform the City’s portion of the work that includes trenching and installing conduits 
and access vaults. As the serving utility, PG&E’s role includes engineering, inspection, and a portion of the construction 
(poles, risers, cables, and connections) needed to complete the undergrounding of electrical lines. As PG&E is the sole 
provider for electrical services, the City must contract with PG&E for successful completion of this work.  A resolution is an 
appropriate way to document the contract with PG&E in lieu of competitive bidding for the project, as authorized by 
Municipal Code section 3.60.050(b).
The total cost of the PG&E work is $186,685.48, with the City having paid a $10,000 deposit during the design phase in 
September 2019. The balance due to PG&E is $176,685.48. 
Staff therefore recommends approving the Agreement with PG&E to allow City staff to compensate PG&E for the 
remaining agreement amount of $176,685.48 to advance the Bermuda Drive Utility Relocation Project.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are available for this PG&E work from the Bermuda Drive Bridge Replacement Project, Project No. 462250.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
84 of 2271

--- page 85 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

CITY OF SAN MATEO 
RESOLUTION NO.  
 (2024)
A RESOLUTION APPROVING AN AGREEMENT WITH PACIFIC GAS & ELECTRIC (PG&E) FOR THE BERMUDA 
DRIVE UTILITY RELOCATION PROJECT
WHEREAS, the Bermuda Drive Bridge Replacement Project (“Project”) is currently in the design 
phase and intended to address identified structural deficiencies of the bridge; and
WHEREAS, existing overhead PG&E electrical lines impede construction of the Project 
because they prohibit pile installation equipment to be raised to install bridge piles; and
WHEREAS, the Bermuda Drive Utility Relocation Project (“Subproject”) was formed wherein 
the City and PG&E will work collaboratively to underground the electrical lines prior to Project 
construction in a joint effort to allow for the pile installation; and 
WHEREAS, as part of the Subproject, PG&E is required to provide engineering, inspection, 
and specific construction services necessary to complete the utility undergrounding; and
WHEREAS, the City is required to enter into an agreement with PG&E and compensate PG&E 
in an amount of $186,685.48 for PG&E’s portion of the work; and
WHEREAS, on September 25, 2019, the City paid a deposit of $10,000 for PG&E’s portion of 
the work and a balance of $176,685.48 remains to be paid; and
WHEREAS, pursuant to San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.050(b), agreements may be 
awarded without competitive bids if calling for bids would be impracticable, unavailing, or 
impossible; and
WHEREAS, City staff recommends approval of an agreement with PG&E in order to 
underground electrical lines prior to construction of the Project.
NOW, THEREFORE THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY 
FINDS and RESOLVES that:
1.
The execution of an Agreement with PG&E to underground electrical lines is 
categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the 
operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of 
existing public or private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or 
topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. 
(CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
2.
In accordance with Municipal Code section 3.60.050(b), calling for bids would be 
impracticable, unavailing, or impossible because: a) PG&E is the owner of the electrical 
lines requiring undergrounding; b) PG&E  is the serving utility and its services for the 
Subproject includes engineering, inspection, and a portion of the required 
86 of 2271

--- page 87 ---
construction; and c) no other organization is permitted to conduct the work required 
for the Subproject.
3.
The related agreement with PG&E to compensate the remaining $176,685.48 for their 
portion of the work for the Bermuda Drive Utility Relocation Project is approved.
4.
The Public Works Director is authorized to sign and execute the agreement in 
substantially the form attached.

City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Parks and Recreation Department
MEETING DATE:
May 20, 2024 
SUBJECT:
Summer Children’s and Youth Theater Program – Agreement
 
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve an alternative purchasing procedure and approve an agreement with Bay Area Educational 
Theater Company to provide children’s and youth theater programs during the summer camp programing from June 16, 
2024 through August 31, 2025 in an amount not to exceed $254,000; and authorize the Director of Parks and Recreation to 
execute the agreement and approve change orders up to $25,000 in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
Bay Area Educational Theater Company (ETC) is a local non-profit organization that provides youth educational and 
production theater experiences. Established in 1996, they have provided summer theater programs for San Mateo Parks 
and Recreation Department for 20+ years. They provide quality experiences and productions and have developed a loyal 
following of families. 
 
San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.070(h)(3) authorizes the City Council to award contracts that are in the City’s best 
interest using alternative purchasing procedures. This is a negotiated contract based upon the ETC’s instructional abilities, 
expertise in the subject field, and marketability to potential patrons, thus qualifying this contract to be awarded using 
alternative purchasing procedures. 
 
Payment terms include two items. First is the core part of the contract, which calls for 70% of the course fee proceeds to 
be paid to ETC, excluding a $10.00 per patron administrative fee. This is in the range of typical recreation instructional 
services in San Mateo and surrounding agencies. Second, an additional materials fee is charged to registrants, and 100% of 
this is paid to ETC to cover their direct costs for materials.
BUDGET IMPACT:
It is anticipated that gross revenues will be approximately $175,000 per year including material fees. Payments to ETC will 
be approximately $127,000, resulting in net revenue to the City of approximately $48,000. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical 
change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

APPROVING ALTERNATIVE PURCHASING PROCEDURE FOR AN AGREEMENT WITH BAY AREA 
EDUCATIONAL THEATER COMPANY (ETC) FOR YOUTH SUMMER THEATER CAMPS 
WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo Parks and Recreation Department has a desire to continue providing 
quality youth summer theater camps; and
WHEREAS, the Bay Area Educational Theater Company has been providing successful youth theater 
camps for the City of San Mateo Parks and Recreation Department for over 20 years; and
WHEREAS, Municipal Code Section 3.60.070(h)(3) authorizes the City Council to approve alternative 
purchasing procedures if in the best interest of the City; and 
WHEREAS, City staff recommends approval of the agreement with Bay Area Educational Theater 
Company in the amount up to $254,000 for services to be rendered between June 16, 2024 through August 31, 
2025 and authorizes the Director of Parks and Recreation to execute the agreement in substantially the form 
presented and approve change orders up to $25,000.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY FINDS and 
RESOLVES that:
1. This agreement is not a project as defined by the California Environmental Quality Act in that it is an 
organizational or administrative activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the 
environment.
2. Pursuant to Municipal Code Section 3.60.070(h)(3), City Council approves an alternative purchasing 
procedure as it is in the best interest of the City to continue offering a well-established, successful youth 
summer theater programs.
3. The agreement with Bay Area Education Theater Company for the Summer Children’s and Youth 
theater, in the amount of $254,000 was negotiated on terms that meet both the City’s and ETC’s interests and it 
is approved.
4. The Parks and Recreation Director is authorized to execute the agreement in substantially the form 
submitted and approve change orders up to $25,000, on behalf of the City.

students have enrolled in the course as set forth in Exhibit A.

Recreation Department and to assist with publicity by giving demonstrations or participating in
Department shows, fairs and special events.

either through the Department, or a partner city in a jointly offered program.

are registered, and registration shall be done on a Department standard registration form with
the participant's or parent/guardian signature.

to the participant’s enrollment or participation in the specific activity in which they are enrolled.

unless specifically approved by the CONTRACTOR’S city representative. CONTRACTOR may
provide participant, or parent of participant, if participant is a minor; the opportunity within the
classroom setting to voluntarily provide their contact information to CONTRACTOR for other
purposes.

A. Coverage shall be at least as broad as:
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL): Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 covering CGL
on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & advertising injury, with
limits no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence ($2,000,000 if required above). If a general aggregate limit
applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or the general
aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and Employer’s
Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury or disease.
3. Proof of Automobile Insurance for the vehicle the Contractor will use as required by the State of
California. If the Contractor’s vehicle changes, it is the Contractor’s responsibility to update the proof of
automobile insurance provided to the City.
4. Sexual Abuse or Molestation (SAM) Liability: If the work will include contact with minors, and the CGL
policy referenced above is not endorsed to include affirmative coverage for sexual abuse or molestation,
Contractor shall obtain and maintain a policy covering Sexual Abuse and Molestation with a limit no less
than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim.
If the Contractor maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and shall be
to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the contractor.

--- page 102 ---
The CGL policy is to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:
1. Additional Insured Endorsement
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as additional
insureds on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations performed by or on behalf
of the Contractor including materials, parts or equipment furnished in connection with such work or operations.
General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an endorsement to the Contractor’s insurance (at least
as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used).
The additional insured endorsement should be a separate document from the certificate and should
indicate: “The City of San Mateo, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are named as
additional insured.”
2. Primary & Non-Contributory Endorsement
For any claims related to this contract, the Contractor’s CGL coverage shall be primary insurance as
respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any insurance or self-insurance
maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or agents shall be in excess of the
Contractor’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.
3. Waiver of Transfer (Subrogation) Endorsement
Contractor hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said
Contractor may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance. Contractor
agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to affect this waiver of subrogation, but this provision
applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of subrogation endorsement from the
insurer.
The Waiver of Transfer (Subrogation) Endorsement should be a separate document from the certificate
and should indicate: “The City of San Mateo, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are
named as additional insured.”
C. Additional Insurance Information
Notice of Cancellation
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be cancelled, except after thirty (30)
days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City.
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may require
the Contractor to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof of ability to pay
losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within the retention.
Acceptability of Insurers
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless otherwise
acceptable to the City.
Verification of Coverage
Contractor shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements, or copies of the
applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this Exhibit. All certificates and endorsements are to
be received and approved by the City before work commences, at least 30 days before programming begins.
However, failure to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the Contractor’s
obligation to provide them. Failure to provide required insurance documents may result in cancellation or
postponement of scheduled programming.
The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all required insurance policies, including
endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.
Reviewed and approved by City Attorney’s Office as of 4/18/2024

May 20, 2024 
SUBJECT:
2nd & El Camino Garage Improvements – Amendment 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Amendment No. 2 with ZFA Structural Engineers for additional structural investigation services at the 2nd and El 
Camino Garage in the amount of $45,201.50, for a revised total amount of $197,441.50; and authorize the Public Works 
Director to execute the amendment in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
The 2nd & El Camino Garage has served the San Mateo downtown community for over seventy-five years. It is a concrete 
structure that has 385 parking spaces spanning five and a half stories. In 2022, ZFA Structural Engineers (ZFA) performed a 
structural investigation which identified exposed, ruptured, and corroded post-tension beam tendons in various areas and 
recommended that repairs be made to restore the integrity of the structure. In 2023, the City developed a two-phased 
approach to address structural issues. Phase 1 includes post-tension Beam Tendon Repairs, which includes the 
replacement of post-tension beam tendons in multiple areas. Phase 2 includes Parking Garage Structural and Safety 
Improvements, which includes the replacement of the stairs, improvements to the concrete surfaces, rebar, lighting, 
vehicle barriers, exterior façade, public sidewalk, signage, striping, waterproofing, and security system, and 
implementation of the City’s Downtown Parking Garage Pedestrian Safety Plan. The design documents for the first phase 
of repairs to the post-tensioned beam tendons were produced in 2023 and the project was awarded for construction in 
January 2024. Design documents for the Phase 2 repairs are currently in production.
In February 2024, City Council approved a professional services agreement with ZFA in the amount of $139,287.50, with a 
$21,000 contingency reserve, for full-time engineering and monitoring during the construction phase to observe, identify, 
and document structural changes as they occur. In early April 2024, Amendment No. 1 was executed increasing the 
compensation to ZFA in the amount of $12,952.50, for a total agreement amount of $152,240.00, utilizing existing 
contingency reserves in order to perform a laser level survey while the structure was unloaded to serve as a baseline for 
ongoing monitoring support during construction.  A contingency reserve remains in the amount of $8,047.50.  
In late April 2024, new cracks were discovered at critical structural beam locations, elevating the need to immediately 
evaluate the integrity of the structure at each level of the parking garage. Based on ZFA’s expertise and extensive 
experience with this specific facility, ZFA was immediately directed to provide additional services to perform scanning, 
sampling, testing, and recommending a solution to mitigate further damage in these locations. The scope and fee of these 
services were completed beyond the previously authorized agreement, scope, and fee, and were necessary to immediately 
complete due to the potential safety risks. The remaining contingency is insufficient to cover these additional services by 
ZFA.
Based on the above, staff recommends approving Amendment No. 2 to include the additional scope and increased 
107 of 2271

--- page 108 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
compensation to ZFA in the amount of $45,201.50, for a revised total amount of $197,441.50.
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is sufficient funding available in the Parking Facility Structural Repairs (46F025) project to fund this amendment. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

SUBJECT:
Casanova Park Restroom Remodel – Contingency Increase
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an increase to the contingency reserve associated with the Newton Construction & Management, Inc. 
construction contract for the Casanova Park Restroom Remodel in the amount of $75,000, for a revised total contingency 
reserve of $120,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to issue change orders within the contingency amount.  
BACKGROUND:
In 2016, the Public Works Facilities Division and the Parks and Recreation Department assessed all park restrooms and 
developed a long-term plan for phased remodeling, based on greatest need and available funding. The Beresford, Los 
Prados, Martin Luther King, Bayside, and Parkside restrooms included in Phases 1, 2 and 3 were remodeled between 2016 
and 2017. The park restrooms included in Phase 4 of the remodeling program are located at Indian Springs, Casanova, and 
Fiesta Meadows parks. 
City Council awarded three separate construction contracts to Newton Construction & Management, Inc. (Newton) for the 
Indian Springs restroom in May 2022, Casanova restroom in October 2022, and Fiesta Meadows restroom in February 
2023. Although each restroom location has unique needs, the general scope of work for each location includes the 
installation of new wall panels, doors, accessibility improvements, new fixtures, partitions, flooring, lighting, a drinking 
fountain, and painting.   
Due to the level of unforeseen conditions at the Indian Springs and Casanova locations, staff instructed Newton to delay 
the start of construction at Fiesta Meadows in the event that the construction contingencies established were deemed 
insufficient to complete work at the first two locations. Fortunately, Indian Springs concluded in early 2024, but the 
renovations at Casanova are projected to exceed the construction contract and contingency amount of $343,253.22 due to 
unforeseen existing conditions that were found to be non-code compliant. These unexpected Casanova costs exceed the 
remaining available project budget in the Park Restroom Refurbishment Phase IV Project fund. The Casanova contract is 
currently scheduled for completion in June 2024.
To manage the budgets, staff coordinated with Newton to terminate the Fiesta Meadows Park Restroom Remodel 
construction contract, which Newton was agreeable with. The Fiesta Meadows remodel will then be recommended for 
award at a forthcoming Council meeting. The contract termination allowed for $198,780.47 to return to the Park Restroom 
Refurbishment Phase IV Project fund, which allows for available funding for the completion of the Casanova Park Restroom 
Remodel construction project.  
Based on the above, staff recommend increasing the contingency reserve associated with the Casanova Park Restroom 
Remodel construction contract by $75,000 to address the additional scope and complete the project as scheduled. 
112 of 2271

--- page 113 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
BUDGET IMPACT:
With the termination of the Fiesta Meadows Park Restroom Remodel construction contract, there is sufficient funding 
available in the Park Restroom Refurbishment Phase IV Project (46F002) to fund the contingency increase.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15301.)

SUBJECT:
Police Department and City Clerk’s Office – Classification Updates
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to 1) add 1.0 Full Time Equivalent Senior Management Analyst position in the Police Department; 2) 
update the job specification for the classification of Senior Management Analyst; 3) increase the salary range for the 
Deputy City Clerk classification; and 4) update the job specification for the classification of Deputy City Clerk.
BACKGROUND:
Police Department - Add 1.0 FTE Senior Management Analyst and update the job specification for the classification:
For the past two years, the Police Department has implemented the Community Wellness and Crisis Response Team 
(CWCRT), which included the department paying for a clinician to be provided by the local non-profit, Star Vista, and 
embedded at the PD for crisis response alongside police officers. The CWCRT has been a resounding success, and the 
addition of the clinician has improved and expanded how the PD meets the behavioral health crisis needs of the San 
Mateo community. At the same time, as part of separate initiatives, the PD has implemented a Homeless Outreach Team 
(HOT), an additional CWCRT clinician, and is in the process of bringing on a domestic violence victim advocate through 
another local, non-profit, Community Overcoming Relationship Abuse (CORA) – to be completed in the Fall of 2024, to 
optimally serve the community. 
Given this increase of civilian, alternative response units, the PD has recognized the important need to add a Senior 
Management Analyst position to help oversee, manage, and coordinate these programs and employees, both internally at 
the PD and also with the partner participating agencies. By adding this position, the PD will ensure proper management of 
these units and programs, appropriate span of control in oversight, and, with the update to the job specification, the 
Senior Management Analyst will have the background clearance required and driving ability to go out into the field to 
oversee the employees of these civilian units as appropriate. The proposed resolution recommends adding 1.0 FTE Senior 
Management Analyst position in the PD, and revising the Senior Management Analyst job specification to reflect the duties 
and required license and background checks when assigned to the PD.
City Clerk’s Office – Increase the Deputy City Clerk salary range and update the job specification for the classification:
The Deputy City Clerk position serves as second in command for the City Clerk's Office and, in the absence of the City Clerk, 
is expected to perform the duties of the Department Head. The duties and responsibilities of the Deputy City Clerk position 
have evolved from the original intent, and has become a position requiring specialized skills, experience, and ongoing 
professional development. The proposed resolution recommends revising the job specification to accurately reflect the 
essential duties and minimum qualifications of the classification.
The compensation study conducted by Bryce Consulting in February 2024 using the labor market survey agencies listed in 
the City’s Management Association Memorandum of Understanding to set the top salary range showed the Deputy City 
114 of 2271

--- page 115 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Clerk position is 36.77% below labor market median in maximum base salary. Consistent with the City’s compensation 
philosophy to pay competitive salaries in order to attract and retain highly qualified employees, the proposed resolution 
recommends setting the monthly salary range of the Deputy City Clerk to match the Senior Management Analyst at 
$9,743.85 - $11,624.18. This would result in a 34.09% salary range increase for the Deputy City Clerk classification effective 
May 26, 2024.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The Deputy City Clerk salary increase will have no impact in fiscal year 2023-24. For fiscal year 2024-25, the total estimated 
compensation increase will be $43,000. Any future costs will be built into the City Clerk’s personnel budget. Adding the Sr. 
Management Analyst to the Police Department will not have any impact in fiscal year 2023-24 as it will not be possible to 
complete the recruitment process before the end of the current fiscal year. Future costs for this position are estimated to 
be $193,000 for fiscal year 2024-25, which will be included in the department’s personnel budget, and will be offset by a  
reduction of contract services within the Police Department’s operating budget, resulting in no additional impact to the 
General Fund. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
These classification updates are not subject to CEQA, because they are government fiscal activities that will not cause a 
physical change in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

             Exhibit B – Deputy City Clerk salary range

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. __ (2024)
CLASSIFICATION UPDATES
POLICE DEPARTMENT AND CITY CLERK’S OFFICE
WHEREAS, pursuant to the San Mateo Municipal Code Section 2.57.030(d), approval of new 
classifications and related job specifications, salary schedules, compensation changes, elimination or 
reinstatement of positions, reallocation of positions, reclassifications, and revisions of job specifications 
must be approved by City Council Resolution; and
WHEREAS, the proposed classification updates as detailed below are in response to the Police 
Department’s requests to add 1.0 FTE Senior Management Analyst position and revise its job specification;  
and the City Clerk’s requests to increase the salary range, and revise the job specification, for the 
classification of Deputy City Clerk; and
WHEREAS, the proposed job specifications for the classifications of Senior Management Analyst 
and Deputy City Clerk are attached hereto as Exhibit A, and the proposed salary range for the classification 
of Deputy City Clerk is attached hereto as Exhibit B; and all exhibits are incorporated by reference; and
WHEREAS, the employee associations representing the impacted classifications have been 
informed of the classification updates and have not brought forth any objections or concerns.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY 
RESOLVES that: 
1.
The classification updates are not subject to CEQA because it can be seen with certainty that 
they will not cause a physical change in the environment; and
2.
The addition of 1.0 FTE Senior Management Analyst position in the Police Department are is 
approved; and
3.
The revisions to the job specifications for the classifications of Senior Management Analyst and 
Deputy City Clerk attached hereto as Exhibit A are approved; and
4.
The salary range increase to the Deputy City Clerk classification attached hereto as Exhibit B 
are is approved.
